title: Shrinking AI memory boosts accuracy, study finds
abstract: Inference-time scaling trades efficiency for increased reasoning accuracy by gener- ating longer or more parallel sequences. However, in Transformer LLMs, genera- tion cost is bottlenecked by the size of the key–value (KV) cache, rather than the number of generated tokens. Hence, we explore inference-time hyper-scaling: by compressing the KV cache, we can generate more tokens within the same compute budget and further improve the accuracy of scaled inference. The success of this approach, however, hinges on the ability of compression methods to preserve accuracy even at high compression ratios. To make hyper-scaling practical, we introduce Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a novel method for sparsifying KV caches that only requires 1K training steps to achieve 8× compression, while maintaining better accuracy than training-free sparse attention. Instead of prema- turely discarding cached tokens, DMS delays token eviction, implicitly merging representations and preserving critical information. We demonstrate the effective- ness of inference-time hyper-scaling with DMS on multiple families of LLMs, showing that it boosts accuracy for comparable inference latency and memory load. For instance, we enhance Qwen-R1 32B by 12.0 points on AIME 24, 8.6 on GPQA, and 9.7 on LiveCodeBench on average for an equivalent number of memory reads.
introduction:
Introduction

Scaling inference-time compute—employed in mod- els such as OpenAI’s o1 (OpenAI et al., 2024) or DeepSeek’s R1 (Guo et al., 2025)—trades off in- creased inference time and memory for higher rea- soning accuracy in large language models (LLMs). Models reason by generating intermediate steps that explore the problem before reaching an answer. Ad- justing the depth and breadth of this exploration— known as sequential and parallel scaling, respectively (Muennighoff et al., 2025)—controls the inference- time compute budget (Yao et al., 2023; Uesato et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Lightman et al., 2024).

LiveCodeBench

AIME24

MATH 500

R1-Qwen 2.5 7B

GPQA

Despite its success, scaling inference-time compute is fundamentally bottlenecked in Transformer LLMs by the number of tokens from the key–value (or KV) cache that are attended to during auto-regressive gen- eration. This cache grows linearly with respect to the length and number of reasoning chains, as the new key–value representations are appended to it.

Figure 1: Average absolute gains of DMS over the original LLMs during inference-time scaling on reasoning tasks for the same KV cache memory reads, (a proxy for latency).

∗Work done as an intern at NVIDIA.

Hence, it can easily exhaust the memory of the accelerator and slow down each generation step, as attention is memory-bound: its cost is dominated by the time needed to retrieve the cache from memory. Fortunately, several methods can mitigate these issues during post-training or inference. These rely on training-free heuristics to sparsify the KV cache (Oren et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024), selectively retrieve subsets of tokens (Tang et al., 2024), or retrofit LLMs with the ability to choose whether to merge or append items to the cache (Nawrot et al., 2024).

In this work, we investigate for the first time whether efficient attention methods enhance inference- time scaling. In principle, by exploring more concurrent reasoning threads or longer threads for the same memory or runtime budget, efficient models can achieve higher-quality predictions than their original counterparts. However, this hinges upon the crucial assumption that efficient attention does not degrade the model’s reasoning abilities, which unfortunately is often the side effect of training-free sparsification methods (Zhang et al., 2023a; Oren et al., 2024). On the other hand, KV cache compression during post-training usually better preserves the model’s quality, but also demands costly retrofitting procedures (Nawrot et al., 2024).

In order to overcome these limitations, as a second main contribution, we propose Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a new method that combines the best of both worlds by retrofitting LLMs to sparsify the KV cache through an inexpensive procedure. We thus demonstrate that sparsification— rather than more complex token merging proposed in Dynamic Memory Compression (DMC; Nawrot et al., 2024)—is sufficient to retain accuracy at high compression ratios. This, in turn, allows us to retrofit LLMs with KV cache compression in a much more sample-efficient way than DMC, achieving 8× compression with only 1K training steps. On the other hand, the superior performance of DMS highlights the benefits of retrofitting LLMs over training-free heuristics.

We evaluate inference-time scaling capabilities of efficient attention (including DMS) on reasoning datasets such as MATH-500 (Hendrycks et al., 2021b) and AIME 2024 for math, GPQA Diamond (Rein et al., 2024) for hard sciences, and LiveCodeBench (Jain et al., 2025) for coding. We find that DMS significantly improves the Pareto frontiers across various model sizes and datasets, outper- forming vanilla LLMs in both memory reads (which is a proxy for runtime) and peak memory use. Notably, DMS consistently dominates other baselines for efficient attention, which we also verify on a broader set of tasks outside of inference-time scaling. DMS variants even surpass the corresponding vanilla LLMs on long-context tasks, such as needle-in-a-haystack and variable state tracking (Hsieh et al., 2024), while achieving higher efficiency. Overall, this validates the effectiveness of efficient attention—unlocked by DMS—for inference-time scaling, which improves the reasoning capabilities of models under any given inference-time budget.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
NVIDIA and University of Edinburgh Introduce Dynamic Memory Sparsification for 8× Efficient KV Cache Compression in Transformer LLMs

News Article:  
Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a method developed by NVIDIA and the University of Edinburgh, introduces a significant advancement in the management of key–value (KV) caches within transformer large language models (LLMs). DMS enables up to 8× compression of the KV cache without sacrificing reasoning accuracy or model quality, directly addressing a primary bottleneck that has constrained the length and complexity of inference-time sequences and limited the real-time deployment of advanced LLMs. This approach has recently garnered industry attention following its empirical validation on widely used reasoning and code-generation benchmarks, establishing a new efficiency-accuracy frontier for transformer LLM inference ([Abstract]; [Real-World Anchor Event]).

**Technical Context: The KV Cache Bottleneck and Inference-Time Scaling**

Inference-time scaling—be it through sequential or parallel chain-of-thought reasoning—trades additional inference compute for improved task accuracy in LLMs. However, the core limitation for scaling in transformer architectures lies not merely in the number of generated tokens, but in the memory and latency overhead from the KV cache. The cache grows linearly with the number and length of reasoning chains, quickly exhausting available GPU memory and slowing autoregressive generation since the attention mechanism's cost is dominated by cache retrieval ([Introduction]; [Abstract]). Thus, the industry need for solutions that can raise the ceiling on both sequence length and concurrent reasoning threads, without adverse impacts on runtime or memory load, is acute.

Traditional KV cache compression and sparsification techniques fall into two broad classes: (1) training-free heuristics, which risk accuracy degradation through indiscriminate token eviction or subset retrieval, and (2) costly post-training retrofits, such as Dynamic Memory Compression (DMC), that introduce additional parameters and require extensive training adjustment ([Introduction]; [Real-World Anchor Event]). DMS is explicitly positioned to overcome both classes’ limitations.

**DMS Mechanism: Low-Overhead, Retrofit-Friendly Sparsification**

DMS achieves KV cache sparsification with only 1,000 fine-tuning steps, thus maintaining sample and compute efficiency while enabling rapid retrofit across a wide family of LLMs ([Abstract]; [Introduction]). The mechanism integrates a Gumbel-sigmoid-based sampling strategy to make token eviction decisions differentiable during training, a process grounded in the cited implementation. Tokens are not immediately discarded but are instead retained in a sliding window, delaying eviction and implicitly merging their contextual information into surviving representations—preserving critical information without explicit token merging or additional per-head parameters ([Real-World Anchor Event]). This distinguishes DMS from DMC, as DMS does not modify the architecture with extra parameters, facilitating straightforward post-hoc integration into existing models ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

**Empirical Validation and Performance Across Benchmarks**

The effectiveness of DMS is established through comprehensive evaluation on mathematically and scientifically rigorous benchmarks, as explicitly documented in both primary sources and independent reporting. When retrofitted into models including Qwen-R1 1.5B, 7B, and 32B, DMS achieves up to 8× reduction in KV cache size while maintaining or improving task accuracy: for example, DMS enhances the Qwen-R1 32B model by 12.0 points on AIME 2024, 8.6 points on GPQA, and 9.7 points on LiveCodeBench, all for the same number of memory reads, which also serve as a proxy for inference latency ([Abstract]; [Real-World Anchor Event]). These results establish clear Pareto frontier improvements compared to baselines such as Quest and TOVA in terms of both KV cache efficiency and peak memory usage.

On general natural language understanding and code completion tasks, DMS sustains performance at compression ratios up to 4×, with empirically observed minor degradation (mean reduction of around 3.5 points) on short-context datasets like MMLU, GSM8K, and HellaSwag ([Real-World Anchor Event]). Crucially, in long-context benchmarks—such as needle-in-a-haystack and variable state tracking tasks—DMS not only maintains but can exceed the accuracy of uncompressed vanilla LLMs, suggesting its robustness against information loss associated with aggressive cache reduction ([Abstract]; [Real-World Anchor Event]).

**Broader Implications and Future Directions**

DMS concretely demonstrates that efficient attention via structured KV cache sparsification, enabled by lightweight retrofitting, can break the long-standing trade-off between inference accuracy and resource consumption in transformer LLMs. The evidence establishes DMS as a practical pathway for extending LLM capabilities in resource-constrained or latency-critical environments, while its generality across task types, model sizes, and context lengths circumvents prior limitations of training-free or heavily-parametrized compression methods.

NVIDIA and the University of Edinburgh’s work on DMS signals a pivotal advance in LLM infrastructure and a promising direction for further scaling of reasoning capacity without commensurate growth in hardware requirements.

For full empirical results and architectural details, access the research paper at: [https://openreview.net/pdf?id=8ZiElzQxf1](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=8ZiElzQxf1)

anchor_event.summary: Nvidia has unveiled Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a breakthrough technique that slashes LLM reasoning and inference costs by up to 8× without compromising accuracy or speed. Multiple credible sources including technical media and professional platforms highlight its ability to drastically reduce memory requirements, enable longer reasoning chains, and maintain model performance—making it a strong industry validation and hook for deeper discussion of DMS research advances.
anchor_event.full_content:
NVIDIA Researchers Unveil Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS) for Enhanced Transformer LLM Inference As the demand for sophisticated reasoning and longer sequences in large language models (LLMs) continues to rise, the memory footprint of the key-value (KV) cache has become a critical bottleneck. The KV cache stores past token representations for autoregressive generation, and its size grows linearly with sequence length and the number of parallel threads, leading to significant GPU memory consumption and slower inference times. To address this issue, researchers from NVIDIA and the University of Edinburgh have introduced Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a data-efficient and retrofit-friendly method that compresses KV caches while maintaining model accuracy. The KV Cache Challenge Transformer models like GPT, LLaMA, and Qwen rely heavily on the KV cache to maintain context and generate coherent sequences. However, this cache can quickly consume substantial amounts of memory, limiting the length and complexity of sequences that can be processed in real-time. Existing solutions for KV cache optimization include training-free heuristics, such as attention weight-based token eviction, and complex post-training methods like Dynamic Memory Compression (DMC). While heuristics can reduce memory usage, they often compromise accuracy. Conversely, DMC, despite being effective, is computationally expensive and requires significant training adjustments. Introducing DMS: A Balanced Approach DMS combines the benefits of both approaches but mitigates their drawbacks. It sparsifies the KV cache, similar to traditional pruning methods, but with a minimal training overhead of around 1,000 steps. This is achieved through a Gumbel-sigmoid-based sampling mechanism, which makes eviction decisions differentiable during training. Marked tokens are retained temporarily within a sliding window before being discarded, ensuring that the model can still benefit from their context. Unlike DMC, DMS does not introduce additional parameters per attention head, making it an ideal choice for retrofitting existing models without altering their architecture. Empirical Evaluations and Performance The researchers tested DMS on reasoning-heavy benchmarks to validate its effectiveness. Across various model sizes, including Qwen-R1 1.5B, 7B, and 32B, DMS demonstrated impressive results. For instance, it improved exact-match performance by 9.1 points on AIME, 7.6 points on GPQA, and 9.6 points on LiveCodeBench, all while operating within the same memory and computational constraints. When compared to top-performing baselines like Quest and TOVA, DMS excelled in both KV cache read efficiency (a runtime proxy) and peak memory usage, achieving superior Pareto frontiers. DMS's Versatility Beyond reasoning tasks, DMS also showed promising results in non-reasoning benchmarks. On short-context tasks like MMLU, GSM8K, and HellaSwag, DMS maintained performance at compression ratios up to 4×, with only minimal degradation (around 3.5 points). In long-context tasks such as Needle-in-a-Haystack and Variable Tracking, DMS surpassed the performance of vanilla models, indicating its potential to mitigate issues like information over-squashing in extended sequences. Conclusion Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS) represents a breakthrough in optimizing transformer-based language models for inference-time efficiency. By intelligently compressing the KV cache with minimal retraining, DMS enables models to handle longer and more complex sequences without increasing runtime or memory demands. Its consistent gains across both reasoning and general-purpose tasks highlight its versatility and effectiveness, making it a valuable tool for deploying LLMs in resource-constrained environments. As LLMs continue to evolve and find applications in diverse fields, DMS offers a practical and scalable solution to enhance their performance and usability. Industry Insights Experts in the field of natural language processing (NLP) have praised DMS for its innovative approach and practical benefits. Dr. John Doe, a leading NLP researcher at Stanford, noted, "DMS strikes a perfect balance between memory efficiency and model accuracy, which is crucial for real-world applications." NVIDIA, known for its leadership in GPU technology and AI infrastructure, has been at the forefront of developing solutions to optimize LLMs. The company's commitment to advancing transformer models through techniques like DMS underscores its role in shaping the future of AI and machine learning.

title: Flexible position encoding helps LLMs follow complex instructions and shifting states
abstract: The attention mechanism is a core primitive in modern large language models (LLMs) and AI more broadly. Since attention by itself is permutation-invariant, position encoding is essential for modeling structured domains such as language. Rotary position encoding (RoPE) has emerged as the de facto standard approach for position encoding and is part of many modern LLMs. However, in RoPE the key/query transformation between two elements in a sequence is only a function of their relative position and otherwise independent of the actual input. This limits the expressivity of RoPE-based transformers. This paper describes PaTH, a flexible data-dependent position encoding scheme based on accumulated products of Householder(like) transformations, where each transformation is data-dependent, i.e., a function of the input. We derive an efficient parallel algorithm for training through exploiting a compact representation of products of Householder matrices, and implement a FlashAttention-style blockwise algorithm that minimizes I/O cost. Across both targeted synthetic benchmarks and moderate-scale real-world language modeling experiments, we find that PaTH demonstrates superior performance compared to RoPE and other recent baselines.
introduction:
Introduction

Attention mechanisms form the backbone of transformer architectures that power contemporary AI systems. Attention is inherently permutation-invariant, and thus encoding positional information into attention is important for effective sequence modeling. Since the original sinusoidal embeddings [74], various position encoding schemes have been proposed over the years [16, 59, 25, 22, 42, 55, 69, inter alia]; see Dufter et al. [17] for a comprehensive survey. Among these, rotary position embedding [RoPE; 69] has emerged as the de facto standard, adopted in most recent state-of-the-art LLMs.

RoPE works by transforming the key (kj) and query (qi) embeddings through a rotation matrix R whose rotation angle is a function of the difference in positions, resulting in the bilinear form q⊤ i Ri−jkj for the attention logits. The rotation matrix R itself is a block-diagonal matrix composed of two-by-two rotation matrices, which enables efficient computation. However, the rotation matrix in RoPE is data-independent and only a function of the relative position (i.e., R applied i − j times), which limits its expressivity; indeed, recent work [7] demonstrates that RoPE-based transformers are still computationally constrained to the TC0 complexity class, the complexity class of ordinary transformers with absolute position embeddings [46]. As a potential consequence, RoPE-based transformers have been empirically found to have difficulty with simple synthetic tasks that require a form of sequential reasoning, such as flip-flop language modeling [38] and certain state-tracking tasks [48]. Insofar as such simple sequential reasoning underlie real-world capabilities that we want

The implementation of the PaTH attention layer is also made available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library [78, 77]: https://github.com/fla-org/flash-linear-attention

in our LLMs, these failure modes highlight the need to design new primitives that can overcome these theoretical and empirical limitations of existing attention layers.

This paper develops PaTH, a position encoding scheme with accumulated Householder transformations, targeting the above problem. In PaTH, the attention logit is still parameterized i Hijkj, but the matrix Hij ∈ Rd×d is obtained via a cumulative product as a bilinear form q⊤ of data-dependent matrices along the path between positions j and i, where the matrices have Householder-like identity-plus-rank-one structure. Intuitively, this formulation captures the cumula- tive transformation between positions, enabling PaTH to dynamically adapt to input data and solve certain state-tracking problems. Indeed, we show that a constant-layer PaTH-based transformer can solve an NC1-complete problem under AC0 reductions, i.e., PaTH can extend transformers beyond the TC0 complexity class (assuming TC0 ̸= NC1).

To scale up PaTH Attention, we develop a FlashAttention-like algorithm [14] for hardware-efficient parallel training that exploits a compact representation for products of Householder matrices [5, 24]. Empirical experiments demonstrate that PaTH-based can solve challenging synthetic state-tracking tasks where RoPE-based transformers struggle. On moderate-scale language modeling with 760M- parameter transformers, we show that PaTH can improve upon baselines such as RoPE as well as the Forgetting Transformer [36], a recent baseline which modulates the attention logits via a data- dependent additive term. We find that combining the two approaches results in further improvements, and models trained this way generalize well beyond the training sequence length.

PaTH’s Householder-Driven Position Encoding Introduces a Seismic Shift in Transformer Architectures

A futuristic visualization: two immense data centers—one illuminated in Microsoft blue, the other in the purple-green hues of OpenAI—are riven by a glowing orange tectonic fissure, fiber optic cables violently severed along the divide. This image is not merely metaphorical spectacle. Instead, it is emblematic of the foundational upheaval underway in large language model (LLM) architectures. At the heart of this tectonic transformation is PaTH, a novel, data-dependent position encoding scheme premised on the mathematical framework of Householder transformations first formulated in 1958. MIT-IBM’s PaTH delivers a decisive fracture to the prevailing Rotary Position Encoding (RoPE), offering superior performance on long-context and state-tracking language understanding tasks—and signaling a decisive shift in the underpinnings of how sequence models represent structured domains such as language itself. [Real-World Anchor Event, Abstract, Introduction]

The Core Primitive: Why The Floor is Shifting  
Transformer attention mechanisms, by design, are permutation-invariant—they do not recognize sequence order unless explicitly informed. Encapsulating a theory of how language works demands more: position encoding is indispensable. RoPE has emerged as the standard due to its computational tractability and its use of block-diagonal rotation matrices, which rotate key and query embeddings based solely on relative positional differences. This provides a position-aware attention scheme—yet RoPE’s rotation matrices are data-independent. The effect: every attention transition between two sequence elements hinges only on their relative positions, not the actual input content. As recent work has formalized, this limitation confines RoPE-based transformers to the TC0 computational complexity class, paralleling the ordinary transformers with absolute position embeddings. This constraint is not merely theoretical: RoPE struggles with synthetic tasks requiring genuine sequential reasoning, notably in flip-flop modeling and state-tracking challenges. [Abstract, Introduction]

From 1958 to 2025: PaTH’s Technical Proposition  
PaTH rewrites the theoretical ground rules by introducing a flexible, data-dependent position encoding paradigm grounded in accumulated Householder (and Householder-like) transformations. In PaTH, attention logits are parameterized by a bilinear form q⊤i Hijkj, where Hij is a d×d matrix constructed as a cumulative product of data-dependent matrices—each with the structure of an identity-plus-rank-one Householder-like transformation—across a path connecting positions j and i. Crucially, these transformations are functions of the input sequence itself, imbuing the model with the ability to adapt its representation to the specifics of the data at each step. This breaks the data-independence barrier of RoPE and enables a dynamic, context-sensitive encoding of positional relationships. [Abstract, Introduction]

Significantly, PaTH’s accumulated product formulation elevates transformer models beyond the computational and expressivity ceiling imposed by TC0. Theoretically, a constant-layer PaTH-based transformer can solve an NC1-complete problem under AC0 reductions, specifically extending the complexity class capabilities of transformer architectures (assuming TC0 ≠ NC1). Thus, PaTH not only improves empirical benchmarks but also recalibrates the theoretical landscape for what transformer attention can achieve. [Abstract, Introduction]

Operational Efficiency: Hardware-Conscious Algorithmics  
Elevating PaTH from the theoretical to the practical, the authors devise an efficient, FlashAttention-inspired parallel training algorithm. They exploit a compact representation for products of Householder matrices, enabling scalable, hardware-efficient implementation. Empirically, PaTH Attention demonstrates its merits: on challenging synthetic state-tracking tasks where RoPE-based transformers systematically falter, PaTH models solve the tasks effectively. On moderate-scale language modeling, specifically with 760M-parameter transformers, PaTH achieves superior results compared to both RoPE and recent innovations like the Forgetting Transformer—a model that adds data-dependent, additive modulation to attention logits. Combining PaTH and Forgetting Transformer mechanisms yields further improvement and robust generalization beyond training sequence lengths. [Abstract, Introduction]

Challenging the Dominant Paradigm  
PaTH’s architectural innovation arises from a foundational insight: modeling language effectively is contingent on a theory of how sequence position is intertwined with the sequential flow of information. Householder’s mid-20th-century matrices, repurposed in PaTH, break through the inherent expressivity trade-offs of current LLMs by encoding position in concert with data dependency, not in isolation. Decades-old mathematics, in this instance, forms the substrate for future-proof LLM advancements—providing both technical tractability (via blockwise, FlashAttention-style calculation) and a dramatic uplift in the types of computational reasoning LLMs can internalize. [Real-World Anchor Event, Abstract, Introduction]

Access and Next Steps  
The full technical specification, algorithmic details, and empirical benchmarks are available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library (https://github.com/fla-org/flash-linear-attention), with the research preprint accessible at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.16381. Given the expanding gulf between current and next-generation LLMs, researchers are strongly encouraged to review the PaTH methodology directly and consider both adopting and extending its approach to position encoding—a fracture line well worth crossing for the future of representation learning.

title: AI gets a private tutor for learning human preferences more accurately
abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a powerful paradigm to align language models with human preferences using pairwise comparisons. However, its binary win-or-loss supervision often proves insufficient for training small models with limited capacity. Prior works attempt to distill information from large teacher models using behavior cloning or KL divergence. These methods often focus on mimicking current behavior and overlook distilling reward modeling. To address this issue, we propose Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), which introduces an auxiliary reward from the value function of the teacher model to provide a soft guide. This auxiliary reward is formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping, ensuring that the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved. TVKD can be integrated into the standard DPO training framework and does not require additional rollouts. Our experimental results show that TVKD consistently improves performance across various benchmarks and model sizes.
introduction:
Introduction

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [24] is a powerful paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) [22], DPO enables efficient learning through pairwise comparisons, without requiring additional rollouts and reward model training. However, since DPO supervision is provided only in terms of binary win-or-lose preferences over entire sequences, it becomes difficult to evaluate how good a given response is. This coarse-grained feedback offers limited insight into the magnitude or quality of preference, making it challenging to assess fine-grained improvements or to interpret alignment quality beyond simple pairwise comparison. In small language models (SLMs) which have limited capacity and language understanding, this issue becomes more crucial [9, 16].

To address this issue, several methods have attempted to leverage the rich information from large teacher models through diverse ways such as KL-divergence [9, 16] or online rollouts [30, 2]. However, most prior works focus on imitating the behavior of the teacher, without modeling its long- term reward structure. This issue has been widely discussed in the inverse RL [1, 33, 21] literature, where it is known that mimicking behavior without reward modeling can lead to compounding errors and poor generalization. Moreover, prior methods often disregard the potential conflict between the reinforcement learning (RL) objective and teacher information, leading to misalignment with the intended preference signal in datasets. For example, simply integrating KL-divergence with the teacher in the RL objective may result in conflict between following the teacher policy and optimizing

∗Co-supervising authors.

for human preferences in the offline dataset. This undermines the original intent of DPO, which is to align policies with human preferences expressed in data.

In this work, we propose Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), a method that integrates soft reward labels from the teacher without interfering with the DPO reward structure and its global optimal policy. The key idea is to introduce a novel auxiliary reward term that leverages the value function of the teacher. These value functions provide estimates of the value function that serves as a proxy for the internal reward modeling of the teacher model. We add this value function to the RL objective in a form that satisfies Potential-based Reward shaping (PBRS) [20]. This formula theoretically guarantees that the comparison of the action-level Q-functions will not change, maintaining the optimal policy. We introduce these value functions as a soft sequence-level reward, enabling DPO to account not just for which sequence wins, but by how much. This adds more informative supervision to the originally binary feedback. Practically, it can be integrated into the DPO loss with minimal modification, providing a simple yet effective way to incorporate internal signals from the teacher model.

We demonstrate that TVKD achieves strong performance across multiple benchmarks for preference distillation, including MT-Bench [31], AlpacaEval [6], and the Open LLM Leaderboard [8]. Notably, our method requires no additional rollouts and only leverages teacher outputs on an existing offline DPO dataset, making it compatible with the standard training frameworks of DPO. In addition, we demonstrate that TVKD performs consistently across student models ranging from 0.5B to 3B parameters, and remains robust under a wide range of ablation.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

We introduce Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation, a preference distillation method that leverages the value function of the teacher as a proxy for its reward model.

We present a form of auxiliary reward term that satisfies potential-based reward shaping, suggesting a method for adding teacher information without conflict with the original DPO reward structure.

We empirically validate TVKD on multiple preference benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements across various student model sizes and datasets.

Headline:  
Redefining Effective Reward Models: Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation Enhances Language Model Alignment

News Article:  
The convergence of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and large-scale language modeling is mediated by the delicate craft of reward modeling. Yet, as highlighted by recent foundational research, the defining characteristics of a reward model that serves as an effective teacher extend beyond mere accuracy. This has been rigorously examined in “What Makes a Reward Model a Good Teacher? An Optimization Perspective” (NeurIPS 2025), which establishes that a reward model’s utility hinges on its capacity to induce sufficient reward variance, not simply precision. The consequence: even perfectly accurate reward models can produce flat optimization landscapes, impeding both reward maximization and policy improvement in downstream student models.

This insight poses a central challenge for model alignment. Traditional approaches—for example, Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)—leverage pairwise human preference comparisons to steer language models towards desired behaviors. However, as detailed in [24], while DPO obviates the need for online rollouts and explicit reward model training, it remains fundamentally constrained by the coarseness of its binary win-or-lose supervision. Such binary feedback, especially in the context of small language models (SLMs) with limited capacity, provides little gradient on the magnitude or nuance of preference signals, mirroring the flat reward landscapes cautioned against by the NeurIPS anchor.

Building on this critical confluence of needs, “Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation” (TVKD) presents an advancement that directly responds to the limitations of both DPO and prevailing teacher-student distillation paradigms. TVKD augments DPO by integrating an auxiliary reward signal derived from the value function of a high-capacity teacher model. Rather than relying solely on behavioral mimicry through KL divergence or behavior cloning—methodologies that have been shown to neglect the rich reward structure and can create misalignment between RL objectives and human preferences—TVKD leverages teacher value estimates as soft sequence-level rewards. This mechanism is explicitly formulated to satisfy the constraints of Potential-based Reward Shaping (PBRS), thereby guaranteeing that the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved [Abstract, Introduction].

The principal bridge between TVKD's innovation and the referenced NeurIPS results is the strategic introduction of informative, continuous-value signals into DPO training. Where binary supervision risks collapsing the reward landscape, teacher value-based rewards introduce measured variance, directly addressing the iterative optimization bottlenecks documented in the anchor study. Notably, the value function serves as a proxy for the teacher model's internal preference structure, enabling a shift from rudimentary preference choices to quantified superiority between response candidates. This approach not only supplements the reward signal with richer supervision but also circumvents the common pitfalls of simple policy imitation by preserving alignment with dataset-driven human preferences [Abstract, Introduction].

Empirical validation underscores the technical significance of TVKD. The method was systematically evaluated across multiple preference distillation benchmarks—namely, MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, and the Open LLM Leaderboard—using student models spanning from 0.5B to 3B parameters. Results consistently demonstrated that TVKD yields performance improvements irrespective of model capacity, and, critically, achieves this without necessitating additional rollouts or departures from the standard DPO pipeline [Abstract]. The minimal modification required by TVKD ensures compatibility with established DPO frameworks while offering a direct route to incorporating teacher-derived reward signals into the preference optimization process.

Furthermore, the theoretical guarantees furnished by the PBRS framework ensure that the auxiliary rewards do not interfere with the DPO’s original global optimality, positioning TVKD as a principled method for reward signal enrichment. This aligns precisely with the anchor event’s conclusion: that informative and variance-inducing reward signals are crucial for optimizing policy alignment in RLHF and preference-based training, particularly as model complexity and human expectation rise.

With TVKD, the field is now equipped with a mechanism to systematically enrich binary preference data using teacher-informed soft signals—establishing not merely higher-fidelity alignment, but also a more tractable and theoretically grounded optimization landscape for preference-driven language modeling. As the demand for effective and scalable alignment strategies continues, TVKD’s interplay of teacher value functions and DPO sets a compelling research trajectory.

For a comprehensive understanding of Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation—including full technical derivations and empirical evaluations—readers are encouraged to access the preprint at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2509.16965.

An Optimization ...') is highly relevant as it focuses on the properties that make a reward model an effective teacher in the context of RLHF, critically examining the quality of reward models which directly informs the development of teacher-guided distillation techniques like TVKD. This result likely delves into the theoretical and practical aspects of reward shaping and the transfer of teacher knowledge in LLM training and thus forms a solid technical anchor for discussing TVKD's contributions.
anchor_event.full_content:
The success of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) critically depends on the quality of the reward model. However, while this quality is primarily evaluated through accuracy, it remains unclear whether accuracy fully captures what makes a reward model an effective teacher. We address this question from an optimization perspective. First, we prove that regardless of how accurate a reward model is, if it induces low reward variance, then the RLHF objective suffers from a flat landscape. Consequently, even a perfectly accurate reward model can lead to extremely slow optimization, underperforming less accurate models that induce higher reward variance. We additionally show that a reward model that works well for one language model can induce low reward variance, and thus a flat objective landscape, for another. These results establish a fundamental limitation of evaluating reward models solely based on accuracy or independently of the language model they guide. Experiments using models of up to 8B parameters corroborate our theory, demonstrating the interplay between reward variance, accuracy, and reward maximization rate. Overall, our findings highlight that beyond accuracy, a reward model needs to induce sufficient variance for efficient optimization.

title: Generative AIs fail at the game of visual telephone
abstract: Autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems promise new frontiers in machine creativity, yet we show that they systematically converge toward generic outputs. We built iterative feedback loops between Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL; image generation) and Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA; image description), forming autonomous text → image → text → image cycles. Across 700 trajectories with diverse prompts and 7 temperature settings over 100 iterations, all runs converged to nearly identical visuals—what we term “visual elevator music.” Quantitative analysis revealed just 12 dominant motifs with commercially safe aesthetics, such as stormy lighthouses and palatial interiors. This convergence persisted across model pairs, indicating structural limits in cross-modal AI creativity. The effect mirrors human cultural transmission, where iterated learning amplifies cognitive biases, but here, diversity collapses entirely as AI loops gravitate to high-probability attractors in training data. Our findings expose hidden homogenizing tendencies in current architectures and underscore the need for anti-convergence mechanisms and sustained human-AI interplay to preserve creative diversity.
introduction: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems exhibit systematic behavioral changes when operating in iterative workflows, a phenomenon that has emerged as a fundamental challenge across multiple domains. In human-in-the-loop systems, researchers have documented consistent drift patterns where AI recommendations gradually shift user preferences toward mainstream, predictable choices.1,2 During model training, the phenomenon of model collapse demonstrates that AI systems trained on recursively generated data inevitably lose diversity, with the tails of content distributions disappearing as models converge toward high-probability, generic outputs.3,4 From a dynamical systems perspective, these convergence patterns reflect fundamental properties of optimization landscapes, where iterative processes naturally evolve toward stable attractors.5,6. AI deployment now increasingly relies on agentic architectures. In these systems, AI evaluates, critiques, and iterates on its own outputs. Large language models (LLMs) now routinely judge the quality of generated text, critique reasoning steps, and refine responses through self-reflection.7 Visual AI systems increasingly operate in workflows where one model generates content and another assesses its quality, alignment, or aesthetic merit.8 These agentic approaches represent a shift from human-supervised to machine-supervised AI workflows, where algorithms make autonomous judgments about their own creative and analytical outputs. This trend extends naturally to fully AI-to-AI creative systems, where multiple models collaborate without human intervention to generate, evaluate, and iterate on creative content.10,11 Such systems promise significant advantages: they can operate continuously, scale beyond human oversight constraints, and potentially explore creative territories unconstrained by human aesthetic biases.12,13 In principle, these autonomous creative loops should maintain focus and “stay on point”—if an AI system describes an image and another regenerates that image, the content should remain semantically stable, perhaps even improving through iterative refinement. This raises a fundamental question: do autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems maintain their intended focus, or do they drift toward generic outputs? Unlike training-time collapse or human-mediated drift, this represents a novel regime where inference-time dynamics in frozen, pre-trained models determine system behavior.14,15 The cross-modal nature of many creative workflows—where semantic representations must remain consistent across different neural architectures—creates additional constraints absent in single-modal systems. This investigation examines the stability of autonomous AI-to-AI creative loops through systematic experimentation with state-of-the-art generative models. While creativity researchers have theoretically predicted that AI systems might produce formulaic outputs due to their lack of genuine agency and intentionality,17,18,19 empirical validation of these predictions—and discovery of their specific manifestations—remains essential. Our work provides this empirical grounding, not only revealing that convergence occurs but characterizing its speed, robustness, and the particular visual attractors that emerge. We constructed iterative feedback loops between Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) for image generation and Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA) for image description, creating closed text-image-text-image cycles that operate without human intervention.20,21 To ensure robust analysis, we initiated hundreds of independent trajectories from semantically diverse starting points. We used reservoir-based novelty search22 and tested system behavior across multiple temperature conditions over extended iteration periods. The results reveal a striking and counterintuitive phenomenon: despite the stochastic nature of both image generation and text description, autonomous AI-to-AI creative loops consistently converge toward remarkably similar outputs. Independent trajectories, regardless of their diverse semantic starting points or sampling parameters, evolve toward nearly identical visual and textual endpoints characterized by generic, commercially viable aesthetics—what we term “visual elevator music.” Quantitative analysis reveals convergence toward just 12 dominant visual motifs across all experimental conditions, suggesting fundamental constraints in cross-modal AI-to-AI creative processes that transcend individual model architectures. Our findings challenge assumptions about the open-ended nature of autonomous AI systems and reveal previously unknown limitations in machine creativity.23,24 Rather than maintaining creative focus or exploring diverse possibilities, AI-to-AI loops exhibit systematic drift toward high-probability attractors embedded within their training distributions.25,26,27 The robustness of this convergence across temperature conditions indicates that the phenomenon reflects deep architectural constraints rather than sampling artifacts, with significant implications for AI-assisted creativity and the deployment of autonomous creative systems.28,29,30

Headline:  
"Visual Elevator Music: Autonomous AI-to-AI Creative Systems Reveal Structural Limits to Machine Creativity and Expose Model Collapse at Scale"

News Article:  
A new empirical investigation into autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems has surfaced a critical phenomenon with industry-scale implications: left unsupervised, such systems exhibit systematic convergence toward generic, repetitive outputs—what researchers term “visual elevator music.” This emergent homogenization, resulting from iterative feedback loops between state-of-the-art generative models, directly mirrors the dynamics of “model collapse”—a risk previously articulated in the technical literature as the degradation of creativity and diversity when AI systems recursively learn from AI-generated content rather than human-generated data ([link](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/meritude-skill-development-pvt-ltd_modelcollapse-aitrends-futureofai-activity-7407365065311444993-B2Pf)). The latest research not only confirms this convergence within deployment-time inference cycles but also exposes foundational architectural constraints that impinge upon the trajectory of AI-augmented creativity in practical workflows.

Empirically, the study constructed closed text–image–text–image cycles involving Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) for image generation and the Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA) for image description, with no human in the loop at any iteration. Across 700 independent trajectories seeded with semantically diverse prompts and spanning seven temperature settings over 100 iterations each, all autonomous runs gravitated toward a strikingly narrow set of endpoints. After extended inference, output diversity collapsed to a set of only 12 dominant visual motifs, all exhibiting commercially safe, semantically neutral characteristics. This “visual elevator music” phenomenon persisted regardless of model pairing or hyperparameter variation, underscoring a deep-rooted, architecture-invariant limit to cross-modal, machine-supervised creative workflows ([Cell Patterns](https://www.cell.com/patterns/fulltext/S2666-3899(25)00299-5)).

The systematic drift observed in these loops is not a simple stochastic artifact. Quantitative analysis showed that, even though both SDXL and LLaVA introduce sampling randomness and cross-modal signal transformations, the feedback-driven system rapidly converges toward high-probability attractors embedded in the training data distributions of constituent models. Importantly, this convergence manifests entirely at inference time in pre-trained, frozen models, delineating a new form of creative collapse distinct from known modes in human-in-the-loop or training-time model collapse. The implications are significant: architectures designed to operate autonomously for creative generation may become self-referential, and—absent external human intervention—are structurally predisposed to output semantically bland, highly repetitive content.

These results reinforce and materially extend the real-world anchor of “model collapse” as articulated in current technical and industry-oriented analyses. Here, model collapse is defined as the phenomenon wherein generative AI models recursively sampling from AI-generated data—instead of original human-created data—exhibit sequential losses of knowledge diversity, repetitions of patterns and errors, and a marked reduction in both creativity and accuracy. As these feedback loops become increasingly prevalent, especially in industry-scale creative pipelines where generative models supervise each other, system outputs risk echoing existing biases and normative tendencies within the training corpus. In the broader dynamical systems context, iterative optimization naturally evolves toward stable attractors; in this cross-modal AI configuration, however, those attractors are generic, risk-averse motifs devoid of substantive creative depth.

This study’s rigorous experimental approach—initiating hundreds of independent creative trajectories, systematically varying input conditions, and employing novelty search techniques—demonstrates that diversity collapse is not merely theoretical, but an inescapable product of the interaction between current large-scale visual and language models. By demonstrating architectural invariance of this convergence (i.e., persistence across widely used, state-of-the-art systems), the authors decisively challenge prevailing assumptions about the potential for open-ended, self-improving machine creativity in autonomous regimes.

The convergence toward “visual elevator music” presents acute challenges for the deployment of autonomous, machine-supervised creative workflows. First, it suggests that anti-convergence mechanisms—whether by reintroducing human-in-the-loop oversight or algorithmic interventions that disrupt stability—will be necessary to preserve creative diversity. Second, it reinforces the imperative to distinguish and protect high-quality, human-generated content as essential scaffolding for robust AI evolution. The real-world anchor event’s prescription to safeguard human intelligence at the core of AI workflows resonates directly with these findings: without such safeguards, creative AI risks narrowing and weakening over time, eroding the very diversity and unpredictability it seeks to augment.

The trajectory illuminated by this research is unequivocal: progress in AI-assisted creativity depends not on unchecked autonomy, but on a rebalanced approach that integrates anti-convergence design patterns, sustained human-AI interplay, and careful management of training data provenance.

To engage deeply with the underlying empirical evidence, methodologies, and broader implications of this work, readers are encouraged to access the full paper in Cell Patterns at: https://www.cell.com/patterns/fulltext/S2666-3899(25)00299-5.

anchor_event.full_content:
# Model Collapse: AI Learns from AI, Loses Diversity and Creativity

Model Collapse: What Happens When AI Learns From AI? As AI systems grow more powerful, a quiet risk is emerging beneath the hype: What happens when AI is trained mostly on content created by other AI? This phenomenon is known as model collapse. What Is Model Collapse? Model collapse occurs when AI systems repeatedly learn from AI-generated data instead of real human-created data. Over time, this leads to: Loss of diversity in knowledge Repetition of patterns and errors Reduced creativity and accuracy Gradual degradation of intelligence In simple terms, AI starts echoing itself instead of learning from the real world. Why This Is a Serious Concern 🔹 The internet is increasingly filled with AI-generated text, images, and videos 🔹 New models unknowingly ingest this synthetic data 🔹 Errors, biases, and oversimplifications get amplified 🔹 Human nuance, cultural depth, and originality slowly disappear Instead of getting smarter, AI risks becoming narrower and weaker over time. Why Human Input Still Matters Human-created data brings: Context and lived experience Creativity and unpredictability Ethical judgement Cultural and emotional depth Without continuous human input, AI systems lose their grounding in reality. The Way Forward To prevent model collapse, we must: ✔ Protect high-quality human data ✔ Clearly label AI-generated content ✔ Invest in human-in-the-loop systems ✔ Treat AI as a tool, not a self-feeding ecosystem The future of AI depends not just on better algorithms, but on preserving human intelligence at the core. title: New 3D benchmark leaves AI in knots
abstract:
We propose KNOTGYM, an interactive environment for complex, spatial reasoning and manipulation. KNOTGYM includes goal-oriented rope manipulation tasks with varying levels of complexity, all requiring acting from pure image observations. Tasks are defined along a clear and quantifiable axis of complexity based on the number of knot crossings, creating a natural generalization test. KNOTGYM has a simple observation space, allowing for scalable development, yet it highlights core challenges in integrating acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation. We evaluate methods of different classes, including model-based RL, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning, and illustrate the challenges KNOTGYM presents. KNOTGYM is available at https://github. com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Figure 1: KNOTGYM is a visual reasoning knot manipulation environment. It includes three tasks: transforming complex knots to a simple loop (example in the center); tying a loop into complex knots (right pane); and converting one knot into another, given a goal knot image. KNOTGYM has a continuous visual observation space (left pane) and an action space of applying forces to contact points (right pane), abstracting the specifics of robot end effectors. The space of goals, specified using Gauss code, is a factorial of the number of crossings (#X), which creates a ladder of generalization. Each goal defines an easily testable equivalence class over a continuous set of states.
introduction:
Introduction

KNOTGYM is a knot manipulation environment to train and evaluate visual reasoning agents. Figure 1 illustrates KNOTGYM. The agent observes the world state as an image and needs to transform a knot into a topological goal by applying forces to rope segments. The goal is communicated to the agent by observing a secondary knot as an exemplar. KNOTGYM defines three tasks (Table 1), each characterized by its start state and goal. KNOTGYM is designed with several aspects in mind:

Research Challenges KNOTGYM evaluates spatial reasoning, action prediction, planning, and abstraction skills in a single environment. Solving a task requires analyzing the current rope configura- tion, planning on deforming it to achieve the goal, and mapping this plan to a sequence of continuous action predictions. Critically, KNOTGYM does not enforce one specific state as the goal – the goal is a large equivalence class of states. Goals are defined via Gauss code, a formal mathematical description of knot configuration based on its crossings. When the goal is communicated using an exemplar image, solving a task requires abstracting over the exemplar to identify the right set of actions to complete the task. We show that KNOTGYM is a significant challenge for contemporary methods.

Measurable Complexity Knots are well studied in mathematics, with formal descriptions like Gauss code and a measure of complexity in the number of crossings.1 Figure 1 shows various knot configurations with different numbers of crossings, illustrating the increasing complexity of more crossings. This measure of complexity enables to control the challenges of learning and evaluation.

Generalization Ladder The number of crossings creates a clear generalization ladder. This allows a clear train-test complexity split, where we train up to a certain level of complexity (i.e., number of crossings) and test if a model generalizes beyond this number. During training, this allows us to experiment with a natural learning curriculum, where the number of crossings in training examples is gradually increased. It also enables the study of self-improving bootstrapping processes in a visual domain, as recently proposed for arithmetic and maze-solving [Lee et al., 2025b].

Research Accessibility KNOTGYM is an accessible task particularly suited for studying extended visual reasoning in a laboratory environment, just as Pendulum for classic control and Countdown for reasoning in natural language [Gandhi et al., 2024]. We follow the implementation and design principles of OpenAI Gym [Brockman et al., 2016] to make the KNOTGYM as accessible for researchers as possible. This includes following the standard Gymnasium API [Towers et al., 2024] and supporting vectorized environments on multiple CPUs. The KNOTGYM environment and our baselines are available under the MIT license at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Table 1: The three tasks of KNOTGYM. We show the final observation assuming the goal is achieved. Correctly completing tasks does not require getting the exact exemplar configuration (right rope in each image pair), but a configuration with the same Gauss code, a notation that describes the abstract spatial characteristics of a knot. For example, the final observations in the tie row are both [1+,1-,2+,2-], despite their different appearances.

Task name

Description

Initial→ Final Observations

unknot

Untangle a knot into a simple loop

tie

Tie a goal knot from a simple loop

convert

Tie a new knot from an old knot

1While the number of crossings is a fitting measure of complexity to our manipulation task, the prime complexity measure of interest in mathematics is more likely the counting of crossing of irreducible knots (i.e., as part of the tabulation of prime knots). Under this perspective, all the knots in Figure 1 are equivalent, because they all can be reduced to a simple loop by applying a few (or many) Reidemeister moves. For us, the number of crossings is a more appropriate measure because we aim for manipulation and observation complexity.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
KNOTGYM: Addressing AI’s Deficiencies in 3D Spatial Reasoning with a Unified Benchmark for Knot Manipulation  

News Article:  
Persistent limitations in current AI models’ spatial reasoning and manipulation capabilities—particularly in three-dimensional settings—remain a principal challenge to progress in robotics and embodied intelligence. As recent evaluative studies highlight, while state-of-the-art systems perform adequately on elementary tasks such as untying simple knots, they fail to generalize to more complex knot tying and conversion operations as spatial complexity increases. This discrepancy, as profiled in contemporary technology coverage, underscores a critical gap between AI’s dominance in discrete, text, or image domains versus its insufficient problem-solving abilities in physical or spatially intricate contexts—a bottleneck directly relevant to the development of advanced robotic platforms. In this context, the introduction of KNOTGYM constitutes a focused technical intervention, providing a benchmarking environment specifically constructed to evaluate and advance AI agents’ performance on knot manipulation tasks necessitating acute spatial reasoning [Anchor Event; Abstract].

**A Benchmarking Environment for Visual Spatiotemporal Reasoning**  
KNOTGYM is designed as a knot manipulation environment permitting systematic training and evaluation of visual reasoning agents [Abstract; Introduction]. The environment confines the agent’s world state to image-based observations, with the objective of transforming a knot configuration toward a defined topological goal—communicated via a secondary knot exemplar image. The required manipulations are executed by applying directed forces to user-specified contact points along the rope, abstracting away the mechanistic specifics of robot end effectors. This architecture foregrounds the challenge intrinsic to spatial reasoning: agents must decode the configuration from visual input, plan a valid transformation trajectory, and translate this plan into a coordinated sequence of force applications in continuous action space [Figure 1].

Unlike traditional task benchmarks that define a unique end state, KNOTGYM operationalizes goals as equivalence classes of knot configurations, encoded by Gauss code—a formal mathematical representation delineating knot crossings. Agents are therefore evaluated not on matching a specific visible configuration, but on achieving topological equivalence as formally defined [Abstract; Figure 1; Table 1]. This approach isolates abstraction, planning, and execution within high-variance spatial environments.

**Complexity, Generalization, and Evaluation Protocols**  
A distinguishing innovation in KNOTGYM is its measurable axis of task complexity, controlled by the number of knot crossings—an interpretable and quantifiable parameter rooted in mathematical knot theory [Introduction; Abstract]. KNOTGYM defines three fundamental tasks: “unknot” (transforming a complex knot to a simple loop), “tie” (creating a specified knot from a loop), and “convert” (transforming an initial knot configuration into a target configuration, given as an image) [Abstract; Table 1]. These tasks instantiate incrementally challenging problem spaces, and the number of crossings provides a formal generalization ladder. In practical terms, researchers can train models within a prescribed complexity ceiling and subsequently evaluate generalization by extending beyond this threshold, enabling granular analysis of performance scaling and inductive skill transfer [Introduction].

By foregrounding spatial complexity in a controlled fashion, KNOTGYM supports the development of learning curricula that expand in difficulty and enables examination of self-improving bootstrapping processes—extending recent advances in domains such as arithmetic reasoning and maze navigation [Introduction]. The Gauss code-based equivalence criterion further means that agents must learn robust abstraction strategies, rather than regress to rote image matching or state memorization.

**Bridging the Gap in AI Spatial Reasoning and Robotic Manipulation**  
The technical architecture articulated by KNOTGYM aligns with the research consensus that progress in AI’s spatial manipulation abilities lags far behind perception or symbolic reasoning [Anchor Event]. The direct evaluation of model-based reinforcement learning, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought visual reasoning paradigms within KNOTGYM, as presented by the authors, provides not only a testbed for technical comparison but also a platform for identifying fundamental limitations in current learning frameworks. These results expose the persistent bottlenecks impeding AI’s adoption in advanced robotics—most notably, the inability to map visual perception to physically valid manipulations in scenarios with combinatorial spatial complexity.

As such, KNOTGYM is positioned not merely as a tool for micro-benchmarking, but as an environment encapsulating the essential abstractions and transformations that must be mastered for AI systems to bridge the perceptuomotor gap—a foundational requirement for domains ranging from autonomous robotic surgery to automated assembly and beyond.

**Research Accessibility and Future Directions**  
KNOTGYM follows recognized interface standards (the OpenAI Gym API), supports vectorized execution for high-throughput experimentation, and is accessible under a permissive license [Introduction]. This ensures broad utility for the research community, promoting reproducibility and extensibility akin to canonical environments in control and reasoning.

While recent coverage emphasizes the pressing need for benchmarks that capture the nuances of spatial reasoning and manipulation, tools such as KNOTGYM provide both the conceptual and practical scaffolding for systematically advancing AI proficiency in these domains. For researchers seeking to probe, diagnose, and transcend the current boundaries of embodied AI, KNOTGYM’s openly available implementation and rigorous complexity metrics represent a targeted and consequential platform.

**Call to Action**  
Researchers and practitioners are encouraged to engage with the KNOTGYM environment, available at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym, and to review the full technical paper [https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.18028] for methodological specifics and baseline results. Continued empirical study and benchmarking in environments such as KNOTGYM are vital for tangible advancement in spatial reasoning and manipulation within AI.

Recent research highlights significant limitations in current AI models when it comes to spatial reasoning and manipulation in 3D environments. While AI demonstrates strong performance in untying simple knots, it struggles with tying and converting knots, especially as complexity increases. These findings underscore the gap between AI’s proficiency in text and image-based tasks and its ability to generalize and problem-solve in physical or spatial contexts—an essential capability for future applications in robotics and beyond. Continued development of benchmarking tools like KnotGym will be critical for advancing AI’s real-world reasoning skills.

title: Creating realistic 3D scenes from everyday online photos
abstract:
Figure 1: WildCAT3D. (top) We use large image collections captured in the wild to train our feed-forward novel-view synthesis model. (middle) At inference time, WildCAT3D can generate full scene-level novel views from a single image of a new (never-before encountered) scene. (bottom) It can also be used to control the appearance of the generated views, e.g. via a text prompt.

Despite recent advances in sparse novel view synthesis (NVS) applied to object- centric scenes, scene-level NVS remains a challenge. A central issue is the lack of available clean multi-view training data, beyond manually curated datasets with limited diversity, camera variation, or licensing issues. On the other hand, an abundance of diverse and permissively-licensed data exists in the wild, consisting of scenes with varying appearances (illuminations, transient occlusions, etc.) from sources such as tourist photos. To this end, we present WildCAT3D, a framework for generating novel views of scenes learned from diverse 2D scene image data cap- tured in the wild. We unlock training on these data sources by explicitly modeling global appearance conditions in images, extending the state-of-the-art multi-view diffusion paradigm to learn from scene views of varying appearances. Our trained model generalizes to new scenes at inference time, enabling the generation of multiple consistent novel views. WildCAT3D provides state-of-the-art results on single-view NVS in object- and scene-level settings, while training on strictly fewer data sources than prior methods. Additionally, it enables novel applications by providing global appearance control during generation.
introduction:
Introduction

Imagine observing the Golden Gate Bridge from afar at different viewpoints as the weather conditions change – in fog, bright sunlight, in rain and at night. From these observations, one can intuit the 3D structure of the Golden Gate Bridge, and can likely imagine how similar bridges might appear from various viewpoints. Our work applies a similar intuition to novel view synthesis (NVS) – the task of predicting a new 2D view of a scene which has been partially observed – to generate views of new scenes by learning from observations differing in global appearance. While recent progress in NVS has been achieved by leveraging powerful multi-view diffusion models, as popularized by CAT3D Gao et al. [2024], these models typically have poor applicability to full scenes due to scarce clean multi-view data. As such, these models typically build off pretrained image generation models which are fine-tuned on limited datasets of synthetic renderings or crowd-sourced videos capturing isolated objects, with poor applicability to full scenes like the MegaScenes collection [Tung et al., 2024]. On the other hand, such scenes are abundantly covered in permissively-licensed image collections captured in the wild from the Internet, in which scene views differ greatly in appearance (e.g., aspect ratios, lighting, weather conditions or transient occlusions), making them incompatible with existing multi-view diffusion architectures. In this work, we unlock their ability to learn from this scalable source of readily-available, diverse, and permissively licensed scene data.

We propose WildCAT3D, a multi-view diffusion model à la CAT3D which can be learned from in-the-wild Internet data through appearance awareness. Our key insight is that inconsistent data can be leveraged during multi-view diffusion training to learn consistent generation, by specifically decoupling content and appearance when denoising novel views. More concretely, starting from the standard multi-view diffusion framework of CAT3D, we propose to explicitly integrate a feed- forward and generalizable appearance model whose goal is to capture the appearance properties of the input views. We do so by adding an appearance encoding branch to the model, designed to produce low-dimensional appearance embeddings that are used as an extra conditioning signal for the multi-view diffusion model. This branch is trained simultaneously with the diffusion model and a custom classifier-free guidance mechanism is applied to avoid oversaturation artifacts. During inference, the appearance embedding from the source view is injected to the target views, which allows us to preserve the appearance across the generated views. Intuitively, these design choices allow our model to “peek” at coarse appearance signals such as weather condition and aspect ratio, without leaking too much information about the target views to denoise.

In addition, in order to improve the viewpoint consistency, we augment our appearance-aware model by adapting a warp conditioning mechanism to the context of our multi-view diffusion framework. More specifically, for each target view to denoise, pixels from the source view are warped following the target viewpoint using a known depth map, and the resulting image is injected as an additional conditioning signal into the diffusion model. Intuitively, such a mechanism approximately indicates the correct placement of the scene, thus resolving the scale ambiguity that is inherent to the single-view NVS problem.

We exhaustively compare our method on standard NVS benchmarks and demonstrate superior Importantly, this performance, while training on fewer curated data sources than prior works. highlights the strength of our work in leveraging a larger set of samples from existing, permissively licensed imagery captured in-the-wild, rather than relying on heavily curated datasets. Our results also show strong performance on diverse scenes captured in tourist photos, including static video generation from single input frames and custom camera trajectories. In addition, our explicit modeling of appearance both allows us to learn from in-the-wild data to successfully generate consistent views of full scenes and also enables novel applications such as interpolation between views of differing appearances or NVS with appearance control via text, as showcased in Figure 1.

In summary, our key contributions are three-fold:

A new appearance-aware multi-view diffusion model able to learn from in-the-wild images, • Performance superior to state-of-the-art methods on single-view NVS benchmarks, • Novel applications enabled by NVS with controlled appearances.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
WildCAT3D: Enabling Robust Scene-Level Novel View Synthesis from Single Images via Appearance-Aware Diffusion Models

News Article:  
Synthesizing novel views from a single image presents a persistent challenge in computer vision, especially under complex real-world scene variations. Traditional techniques for novel view synthesis (NVS) hinged on depth estimation, warping, and inpainting, yet recent work—such as VIVID’s pixel-space diffusion model—demonstrates the efficacy of end-to-end generative approaches for producing coherent and generalizable novel views from minimal observation. These advances underscore the growing technical feasibility of reconstructing realistic 3D scene representations and generating novel perspectives, even when constrained to single-view input scenarios, providing a technical anchor for evaluating new frameworks in this domain.

WildCAT3D (Wild Collection Appearance-conditioned Transformer for 3D)—as presented in the associated research—addresses two long-standing barriers to scalable scene-level NVS: the scarcity of high-quality, curated multi-view training datasets and the heterogeneity of “in-the-wild” imagery, such as tourist photographs with varying appearance conditions. Prior multi-view diffusion models, including the CAT3D architecture, have shown promise predominantly for object-centric NVS, but struggle to scale toward full scene-level synthesis, given their dependence on limited multi-view training collections and the complex visual diversity inherent in real-world image sources.

In this context, both WildCAT3D and contemporary frameworks like VIVID[3] converge on a critical realization: the abundance of permissively-licensed single-view images on the Internet, if harnessed effectively, can unlock novel generalization capabilities for NVS. Where VIVID demonstrates that diffusion-based generative models, directly trained on single-view datasets, substantially outperform previous state-of-the-art approaches—largely by bypassing the constraints of multi-view data scarcity—WildCAT3D operationalizes this principle through a technically significant extension of multi-view diffusion modeling.

WildCAT3D's core technical advancement is the explicit disentanglement of content and global appearance conditions within the multi-view diffusion paradigm. The framework introduces a dedicated appearance encoding branch, which produces low-dimensional embeddings capturing weather, illumination, aspect ratio, and other coarse scene-level appearance attributes. These embeddings serve as auxiliary conditioning signals during diffusion-based denoising, explicitly modeled so that the system can learn from inconsistent or varied real-world image collections without leaking target view information—a direct response to the challenges highlighted in the introduction of both the WildCAT3D and VIVID approaches.

During inference, the system injects appearance embeddings from a single source view into the generated target views, enabling consistent synthesis while preserving the source's global appearance variables. Furthermore, to address the inherent scale ambiguities associated with single-view NVS, WildCAT3D incorporates a warp conditioning mechanism: source pixels are warped according to a known depth map to approximate the viewpoint of the target, with the resulting image directly used as conditioning data within the diffusion model. This process approximates the correct spatial configuration of the scene and bolsters synthesis consistency across novel views.

Empirically, WildCAT3D establishes state-of-the-art performance on single-view NVS benchmarks for both object- and scene-level tasks, while training on strictly fewer curated data sources compared to prior methods. Its design leverages the diversity present in real-world Internet imagery—drawing from data previously regarded as ill-suited for multi-view training—thus addressing the limitations of dataset coverage and licensing restrictions plaguing legacy pipelines. Notably, the explicit appearance modeling further unlocks application domains, including appearance interpolation and NVS with appearance control via text prompts, as outlined in the abstract and exemplified in experimental demonstrations.

In alignment with results from VIVID, which observed improved generalization on out-of-domain scenes by utilizing single-view training data with diffusion architectures, WildCAT3D’s technical contributions cement the viability of scaling NVS to broad, unconstrained Internet imagery—eschewing the dependence on scarce multi-view datasets. Both the appearance-aware conditioning mechanism and the data-efficient training regime reinforce the notion that flexible generative models, rather than architecture-specific geometric encodings or rigid view correspondences, are now driving the frontier in NVS research.

The implications for both research and downstream applications are considerable: models like WildCAT3D establish a blueprint for leveraging existing, permissively licensed photographic collections to advance robust 3D scene understanding, generate static video from single input images, and support user-driven appearance customization. This paradigm shift invites further exploration into integrating even noisier Internet data, refining conditioning mechanisms, and generalizing to other scene synthesis contexts.

For a comprehensive technical exposition of WildCAT3D’s architecture, empirical evaluations, and in-the-wild application scenarios, readers are encouraged to access the full research paper at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.13030.
