The paper’s focus on leveraging diffusion in the pixel space for generating new views from single images offers a technical backdrop for evaluating and contrasting advances like WildCAT3D, especially in handling scene complexity and varied real-world appearances.
anchor_event.full_content:
Abstract: _Synthesizing a novel view from a single input image is a challenging task. Traditionally, this task was approached by estimating scene depth, warping, and inpainting, with machine learning models enabling parts of the pipeline. More recently, generative models are being increasingly employed in novel view synthesis (NVS), often encompassing the entire end-to-end system. In this work, we adapt a modern diffusion model architecture for end-to-end NVS in the pixel space, substantially outperforming previous state-of-the-art (SOTA) techniques. We explore different ways to encode geometric information into the network. Our experiments show that while these methods may enhance performance, their impact is minor compared to utilizing improved generative models. Moreover, we introduce a novel NVS training scheme that utilizes single-view datasets, capitalizing on their relative abundance compared to their multi-view counterparts. This leads to improved generalization capabilities to scenes with out-of-domain content._

title: Researchers reveal bias in a widely used measure of algorithm performance
abstract:
Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection

Normalized mutual information is widely used as a similarity measure for evaluating the In this paper, we argue that results performance of clustering and classification algorithms. returned by the normalized mutual information are biased for two reasons: first, because they ignore the information content of the contingency table and, second, because their symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on algorithm output. We introduce a modified version of the mutual information that remedies both of these shortcomings. As a practical demonstration of the importance of using an unbiased measure, we perform extensive numer- ical tests on a basket of popular algorithms for network community detection and show that one’s conclusions about which algorithm is best are significantly affected by the biases in the traditional mutual information.
introduction:
1 Introduction

A common task in data analysis is the comparison of two different labelings of a set of objects. How well do demographics predict political affiliation? How accurately do blood tests predict clinical outcomes? These are questions about how similar one labeling of individuals/items/results is to another and they are commonly tackled using the information theoretic measure known as mutual information, in which an experimental or computational measure of some kind is compared against a ground truth.

Mutual information works by asking of how efficiently we can describe one labeling if we already know the other [1]. Specifically, it measures how much less information it takes to communicate the first labeling if we know the second versus if we do not. As an example, mutual information is commonly used in network science to evaluate the performance of algorithms for network commu- nity detection [2]. One takes a network whose community structure is already known and applies a community detection algorithm to it to infer the communities. Then one uses mutual information to compare the output of the algorithm to the known correct communities. Algorithms that con-

sistently achieve high mutual information scores are considered good. We will use this application as an illustrative example later in the paper.

Mutual information has a number of appealing properties as a tool for comparing labelings. It is invariant under permutations of the labels, so that, for example, two divisions of a network into communities that differ only in the numbering of the communities will be correctly identified as being the same. It also returns sensible results in cases where the number of distinct label values is not the same in the two labelings. However, the standard mutual information measure also has some significant shortcomings, and two in particular that we highlight in this paper. First, it has a bias towards labelings with too many distinct label values. For instance, a community detection algorithm that incorrectly divides networks into significantly more groups than are present in the ground truth can nonetheless achieve a high mutual information score. A number of approaches for correcting this flaw have been proposed. One can apply direct penalties for incorrect numbers of groups [3] or subtract correction terms based on the average mutual information over some ensemble of candidate labelings [4, 5] or on the statistics of the contingency table [6, 7]. For reasons we discuss shortly, we favor the latter approach, which leads to the measure known as the reduced mutual information.

The second drawback of the mutual information arises when the measure is normalized, as it commonly is to improve interpretability. The most popular normalization scheme creates a measure that runs between zero and one by dividing the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings being compared [8], although one can also normalize by the minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the entropies. As we demonstrate in this paper, however, these normalizations introduce biases into the results by comparison with the unnormalized measure, because the normalization factor depends on the candidate labeling as well as the ground truth. This effect can be large enough to change scientific outcomes, and we provide examples of this phenomenon.

In order to avoid this latter bias, while still retaining the interpretability of a normalized mutual information measure, we favor normalizing by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This removes the source of bias but introduces an asymmetry in the normalization. At first sight this asymmetry may seem undesirable, and previous authors have gone to some lengths to avoid it. Here, however, we argue that it is not only justified but actually desirable, for several reasons. First, many of the classification problems we consider are unaffected by the asymmetry, since they involve the comparison of one or more candidate labelings against a single, unchanging ground truth. Moreover, by contrast with the multitude of possible symmetric normalizations, the asymmetric measure we propose is the unique way to normalize the mutual information without introducing biases due to the normalization itself.

More broadly, the asymmetric measure is more informative than the conventional symmetric one. Consider for instance the common situation where the groups or communities in one labeling are a subdivision of those in the other. We might for example label individuals by the country they live in on the one hand and by the town or city on the other. Then the more detailed labeling tells us everything there is to know about the coarser one, but the reverse is not true. Telling you the city fixes the country, but not vice versa. Thus one could argue that the mutual information between the two eshould be asymmetric, and this type of asymmetry will be a key feature of the measures we study.

Both drawbacks of the standard mutual information—bias towards too many groups and depen- dence of the normalization on the candidate labeling—can be addressed simultaneously by using an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information as defined in this paper. In support of this approach we present an extensive comparison of the performance of this and other variants of the mutual information in network community detection tasks, generating a large number of random

test networks with known community structure and a variety of structural parameters, and then attempting to recover the communities using popular community detection algorithms. Within this framework, we find that conclusions about which algorithms perform best are significantly impacted by the choice of mutual information measure, and specifically that traditional measures erroneously favor algorithms that find too many communities, but our proposed measure does not. Code im- plementing our measure can be found at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Normalized Mutual Information Measures Found Systematically Biased in Clustering and Community Detection Evaluation

News Article:  
A new study published in _Nature Communications_ ([DOI: 10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)) reveals that normalized mutual information (NMI), a principal metric for evaluating clustering and community detection algorithms, systematically introduces bias—an effect capable of distorting algorithmic comparisons and scientific conclusions. This critical finding, announced and contextualized by the Santa Fe Institute, warns that standard NMI may misleadingly favor algorithms that either fragment data excessively or simplify groupings, prompting a reassessment of widely adopted comparative methodologies in data analysis and network science.

At the core of this research lies a technically rigorous analysis of NMI as applied to the comparison of two different labelings over a set of objects. Such comparisons are foundational across domains, from assessing the predictive power of demographic versus political affiliation data to evaluating the fidelity of community detection algorithms on networks with known ground truth. The mutual information metric quantifies the efficiency with which knowledge of one labeling reduces the information needed to specify the other, making it a mathematically grounded tool for measuring label similarity.

Despite several attractive properties—such as invariance under label permutation and applicability when comparing partitions with different numbers of groups—the conventional mutual information approach harbors two critical deficiencies. First, it demonstrates a pronounced bias towards labelings with an excessive count of groupings, inadvertently rewarding algorithms that systematically over-segment datasets ([Abstract], [Introduction]). Second, the standard practice of symmetric normalization (dividing by the arithmetic mean, minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the labelings’ entropies) introduces an additional bias, as the normalization factor depends not only on the ground truth but also on the candidate output—a factor large enough to change the perceived performance hierarchy among algorithms ([Introduction]).

External validation provided by the Santa Fe Institute’s independent reporting underscores the practical ramifications of these findings: “First, it [NMI] can reward algorithms that over-divide data, inventing extra categories and appearing more accurate than they are. Second, commonly used normalization methods can introduce a further bias toward artificially simple algorithms. Both effects can distort comparisons, especially in complex problems where the ‘true’ grouping is not straightforward” ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

To address these intertwined biases, the research team introduces a modified metric: the asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information. Technically, this alternative first applies a correction based on the underlying contingency table statistics, resolving the over-segmentation bias previously noted ([Abstract], [Introduction]). It then normalizes the reduced mutual information by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone, eliminating the dependence on the candidate labeling and thus removing the normalization-induced bias. Notably, this normalization is asymmetric—a feature the authors justify as both necessary and, in many contexts, uniquely appropriate. For standard evaluation settings, where comparisons are generally made with respect to a fixed ground truth, asymmetry does not affect result interpretability and provides a logically sound resolution to the normalization problem ([Introduction]).

Crucially, the study demonstrates the operational significance of this new measure through extensive computational experiments. By assessing the performance of multiple community detection algorithms across a diverse suite of random networks with known community structure and varying parameters, the authors show that algorithm rankings produced by traditional NMI measures can be highly unstable—erroneously favoring algorithms that overfit by finding spurious communities. Their asymmetric, reduced measure, however, delivers a more robust and consistent assessment, free from the unintended incentives and distortions generated by the standard metric ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Real-World Anchor Event]).

The repercussions for algorithm development and fair benchmarking are substantial. As the popularity of community detection and clustering methods continues to drive advances across computational biology, social network analysis, and beyond, the technical community must carefully reconsider the metrics used to gauge algorithmic performance ([Abstract], [Introduction]). The codebase implementing the new metric is openly available at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi, facilitating rapid adoption and critical evaluation by the broader research community.

For researchers and practitioners reliant on rigorous comparison of classification and clustering solutions, the evidence is clear: established normalized mutual information measures can systematically bias and distort comparative outcomes. To advance faithfully, the field is urged to examine the empirical and methodological arguments presented in the full publication.  
**Read the paper “Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection” in _Nature Communications_, [DOI: 10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8), and access the implementation to ensure the methodological integrity of future work.**

It refers to the development of an asymmetric, reduced version of the mutual information metric, which closely aligns with the central arguments and proposals of the primary research, without being the same study. Source [2] thus offers a technically relevant and high-quality external validation and context for the discussed biases and the need for revised metrics.

**Read the paper “Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection” in _Nature Communications_ (December 11, 2025). DOI: [10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)**

First, it can reward algorithms that over-divide data, inventing extra categories and appearing more accurate than they are. Second, commonly used normalization methods can introduce a further bias toward artificially simple algorithms. Both effects can distort comparisons, especially in complex problems where the “true” grouping is not straightforward.

To address these issues, the team developed an asymmetric, reduced version of the mutual information metric that eliminates both sources of bias. When they applied their measure to popular community-detection algorithms, they found that, while standard NMI can point researchers to different “best” algorithms depending on how it’s calculated, their revised measure offers a more consistent and trustworthy comparison.

title: Resolving to spend less time on your smartphone Understanding your travel habits can help, say researchers
abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habits in contemporary society—in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants’ trips for two weeks (N=419 participants; n=27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n=7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.
introduction:
Habits are a direct and deep-rooted driver of human behavior1,2. By automating our cognition, habitual processes can make the world easier to navigate. Yet, they can also conflict with our goals3,4. For better and for worse, the degree to which behavior is performed habitually has powerful implications for how we engage with the world around us. Although people engage in a wide variety of habits in daily life, habits are typically studied in isolation5. By zooming out from this standard scope, we can reveal broader, multiplicative ways that habitual processes underlie real-world behavior across domains of everyday life.

Habitual behaviors are cued and performed automatically after being frequently rewarded in stable contexts2. In turn, the strength of a habit is indicated by the level of frequency (i.e., number of occurrences) and automaticity (i.e., lack of conscious processes)6. In contemporary society, spatial and smartphone habits act as cornerstone habits. Although a variety of factors can contribute to mobility choices and mobile phone use in a given situation, past research across disciplines indicates that habit is a leading predictor of both7,8. Critically, the interaction between these two types of habits is ideally suited for analyzing interactive habitual processes in daily life, as smartphone habits are uniquely activated across spaces9,10. Exploring their interactive relationship is also vital given the growing integration of mobility and technology: a convergence with implications for numerous facets of human behavior11–16.

Spatial habits are a prominent form of habitual behavior, as people often select and engage with their everyday spaces out of habit7,8. Scholars have extensively examined the role of habit in the selection of routes17–21, or how individuals travel from an origin to a destination via a particular mode of transportation (e.g., walking). Here, we focus on the role of habit in how people select routes as well as destinations, which together comprise the activity spaces that people inhabit in daily life22. Thus, spatial habits are comprised of places (i.e., destinations) and how individuals move between them (i.e., routes). Spatial habits can be cued by myriad factors (e.g., time, trip purpose)23. Research in cognitive geography has investigated spatial habits through the study of navigation, spatial cognition, and mental mapping. A notable example is anchor-point theory, which examines how people perceive and represent space through reference points like landmarks or neighborhoods24. Moreover, the centrality of spatial behavior in past habit research reflects the repetitive nature of daily mobility patterns identified in large-scale computational studies25,26. Spatial habits thereby have unique potential to operate as foundational habits: they determine the stable contexts that offer cues (or 'triggers') for other daily habits.

Smartphone habits are another dominant form of habitual behavior in modern society, as well as a heuristic case in habit research27. By bundling many possible behaviors (e.g., social media scrolling, podcast listening) into one handheld object, smartphones facilitate the development of a wide variety of activation cues (e.g., times, user activities), including personalized sets of cues tied to specific apps28. Accordingly, smartphone habits can be uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts9,10—challenging the presumed 'context-dependence' of traditional media habits and presumed role of location for cueing habits at-large2. However, some studies reveal that certain types of apps are also more likely to be used in certain types of places, as routes and destinations afford certain activities29,30. In this way, specific smartphone behaviors may develop implicit associations with specific types of spaces31,32—such as more habitually selected contexts33. In other words, spatial habits have the potential to make smartphone habits stronger.

To test this proposition, we examine whether more habitual apps are used disproportionately more in (1) habitually travelled routes and (2) habitually visited destinations. We test whether habitual apps can be interwoven with transportation habits by evaluating app use on routes. For example, app use and specific spaces may become embedded in scripts34: a habitual route (e.g., driving from home to work) can cue an app habit (e.g., listening to Spotify) at rehearsed points during the trip (e.g., traffic lights). These scripts may also be prompted by specific cues (e.g., time of day, social setting), thereby coupling spatial habits (e.g., the morning commute) and smartphone habits (e.g., listening to a news podcast) through sequences of cued actions. Additionally, we test the downstream influence of spatial habits on smartphone use by evaluating app use in destinations (e.g., the workplace). Since spaces chosen out of habit are more familiar35, habitually visited destinations may be more likely to contain familiar cues that activate app habits (e.g., checking a social media app while cleaning the kitchen). Altogether, we test whether app habits are activated more when individuals choose routes and destinations out of habit.

We further examine the robustness of the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits by exploring the distinctive role of social vs. non-social apps. Social apps provide a direct channel for social needs and norms to be activated habitually in daily life36. Based on the potency of social rewards in habit formation, along with the high proportion of social app usage in studies of overall smartphone usage37,38, social apps are expected to exhibit higher habit strength27,39. The theorized strength of social app habits may derive from their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues40. For example, people may habitually check social apps throughout the day in response to or in anticipation of new messages27,37. Thus, social app habits may be more likely to be context-independent: triggered across a variety of spatial contexts39.

Habits present a unique measurement challenge because they are triggered and performed with minimal conscious awareness throughout daily life41. Traditionally, habit research has centered on establishing the causal role of habit versus more conscious processes such as goals27. Here, moving beyond the question of whether habits or goals matter more, we focus on the real-world conditions—or contexts—under which habits become more likely to be activated. Accordingly, the current study sought to capture habits in the real world. By coupling GPS and app logs with map-based questionnaires, we collected objective and subjective indicators of spatial and smartphone habits and used those indicators to predict app use in context.

Headline:  
Intersecting Habits: Privacy Risks and Behavioral Amplification in Transportation App Usage

News Article:

Recent advances in transportation app technology have significantly increased the volume of sensitive behavioral and location data collected on users, raising acute concerns over data privacy and the downstream implications for habitual app use. A prominent example is Ajusto, a transportation app that accumulates data on driver behavior and travel patterns with the promise of insurance incentives. Such platforms highlight a critical dilemma: robust user engagement is often achieved at the cost of extensive behavioral tracking, and, as documented by Rossi & Musolesi (2014) and de Montjoye et al. (2013), the aggregation of travel habits with other data can facilitate user re-identification, intensifying privacy challenges. Alongside these concerns, behavioral economics tools, such as awarding “experience points” for routine use, actively encourage the habituation of app engagement in transportation contexts. The technical and ethical stakes embedded in these practices form the foundation for a deeper examination of how habitual processes underlie smartphone app usage during everyday travel.

Empirical evidence presented in the study "Multiplicative effects of spatial and smartphone habits on daily behavior" (https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2) directly addresses the intersection between spatial and smartphone habits, revealing a multiplicative relationship that shapes daily patterns of app use. Drawing from a large-scale dataset—comprising 27,446,977 GPS and app logs along with 7,226 trip questionnaires from 419 participants—the authors demonstrate that the likelihood and intensity of smartphone app usage increase significantly along habitually traveled routes and in habitually visited destinations. This effect is most pronounced for objective indicators of habit, such as usage frequency and automaticity, supporting the hypothesis that spatial routines act as robust cues for smartphone habits.

The contemporary integration of mobility and digital technology renders this finding technically significant. The research positions spatial and smartphone habits not merely as co-occurring but as mutually reinforcing: routes and places selected out of habit stabilize contexts that trigger digital routines, while app usage itself is woven into mobility scripts—structured sequences of contextually cued behaviors, such as repeatedly launching a specific app at a specific point along a daily commute. In transportation app ecosystems, the implications are immediate. Applications incentivizing repeated use through behavioral reward structures—exemplified by “experience points” on Waze and Moovit—build upon the automaticity and frequency that underpin habit formation. As app engagement is reinforced in stable, predictable contexts (e.g., daily commutes), transportation apps can leverage spatial habits to consolidate digital routines, raising the potential for systematic amplification of both mobility and smartphone app usage.

This cyclical relationship also brings privacy considerations into sharp focus. Transportation apps routinely log sensitive spatial trajectories, which, when algorithmically correlated with app usage patterns, can reconstruct granular models of user behavior. The study’s methodological contribution—combining continuous mobile sensing with in-situ, map-based questionnaires—illustrates just how precisely habitual processes can now be quantified and contextualized. When platforms systematically reward habitual engagement, the boundaries between voluntary use and automated behavioral tracking blur, intensifying both the habitual strength of app use and the potential scope of data exposure. As noted in transportation systems research, safeguarding user privacy becomes increasingly complex as the granularity and volume of logged behavioral data rise alongside engineered engagement strategies.

A further technical nuance from the study concerns the heterogeneity of app habits. Social apps exhibit habit strengths that are both higher and more context-independent than non-social apps. This distinction is relevant to transportation app design: social app usage is triggered by internal rather than spatial cues and is likely to persist across varied environments. In contrast, non-social app habits—such as those linked to navigation or media streaming during travel—are tightly coupled to spatial routines. This duality underscores the necessity for differentiated privacy protection regimes and habit-intervention strategies, particularly as app designers seek to optimize engagement without exacerbating data vulnerabilities.

These findings necessitate a more comprehensive technical dialogue about the implications of habitual processes in transportation app usage. The convergence of spatial and digital routines suggests that interventions intended to reshape travel behavior—whether for economic, environmental, or public health objectives—must account for the automated, often unconscious activation of smartphone habits within stable mobility patterns. Simultaneously, the privacy calculus for users becomes more complex as their behavioral and spatial signatures become increasingly intertwined and susceptible to third-party aggregation and inference.

As the transportation app ecosystem evolves—driven by both digital innovation and mounting privacy concerns—the technical community is tasked with critically evaluating how habitual processes can be measured, incentivized, and, where necessary, constrained. The study's demonstration of linked spatial and smartphone habits, and their implications for the design and deployment of behavioral interventions in digital mobility platforms, provides a robust empirical foundation for guiding this future work.

Access the original research for a detailed methodological exposition and expanded discussion of its implications: https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2

anchor_event.full_content:
With many transportation apps tracking and storing sensitive information, such as user location data, concerns are emerging on how to safeguard user privacy (King & Raja, 2012) (Saif, Peasley, & Perinkolam, 2015), and on how this might impact usage of transportation apps and services. Ajusto, a transportation app released by Desjardins Insurance, tracks driver behavior in return for a promise of car insurance savings. As with many transportation apps that collect sensitive user data, Ajusto has critics worrying about how user location data are being used, such as the potential for data aggregation with other information on a user’s smartphone and the sharing of user data to third parties (CBC News, 2015). Studies have shown, for example, that an individual’s identity can be discovered purely by compiling travel habits in conjunction with other publicly available datasets (Rossi & Musolesi, 2014), (de Montjoye, Hidalgo, Verleysen, & Blondel, 2013).

1.   Experience points: Points are awarded for simply participating on a regular basis in the app. Waze and Moovit are prime examples of awarding experience points for basic interactions. Such repeated awards create habits of using the app and associated services.

The wide usage of behavioral economics and social psychological principles in apps suggests the importance of understanding the theory and practice of behavioral change in the context of smartphone apps for transportation. Additional work would help to test some of the hypotheses and build on anecdotal evidence that successful behavioral change mechanisms from other sectors can be usefully deployed to change transportation behavior. The next chapter reviews opportunities (e.g., planning and data collection) and challenges (e.g., privacy and digital divide), as well as best practices for deploying smartphone apps.

title: How AI helps solve problems it doesnt even understand
abstract: Streamlining constraints (or streamliners, for short) narrow the search space, enhancing the speed and feasibility of solving complex constraint satisfaction problems. Traditionally, streamliners were crafted manually or generated through systematically combined atomic constraints with high-effort offline testing. Our approach utilizes the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to propose effective streamliners for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language and integrates feedback to the LLM with quick empirical tests for validation. Evaluated across seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, our method achieves substantial runtime reductions. We compare the results to obfuscated and disguised variants of the problem to see whether the results depend on LLM memorization. We also analyze whether longer offline runs improve the quality of streamliners and whether the LLM can propose good combinations of streamliners.
introduction:
Introduction

Streamliners are certain constraints added to a constraint model to reduce the search space, thereby improving the feasibility and speed of finding solutions to complex constraint satisfac- tion problems. By incorporating domain-specific knowledge, streamliners can guide the con- straint solver, allowing it to bypass less promising areas of the search space. Gomes and Sell- mann [2004] introduced streamliners to speed up the constrained-based search for hard combina- torial design problems. Today, streamliners are a standard tool for speeding up constrained-based search. Streamliners are closely related to implied/redundant constraints, symmetry-breaking constraints, and dominance-breaking constraints; however, adding a streamliner may even cause the constraint model to become inconsistent.

Originally, streamliners were hand-crafted by researchers who used their theoretical insight to analyze the constrained model. However, progress has also been made on the automated gen- eration of streamliners [Spracklen et al., 2023] by systematically trying the effect of some atomic constraints, such as imposing specific constraints on integer and function domains, like enforcing odd or even values, monotonicity, and properties like commutativity, as well as facilitating spe- cific attributes in binary relations. These atomic restrictions are tested on thousands of problem instances, and those that show a good streamlining effect are systematically combined.

This paper proposes a different approach to automated streamliner generation that utilizes the generative capabilities (“creativity”) of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate stream- liners, providing a neuro-symbolic prototype implementation StreamLLM, and rigorously tests

it on seven different constraint satisfaction problems using at least 310 CPU days1. Our ap- proach leverages the capabilities of LLMs to infer potentially effective streamliners, similar to an experienced researcher’s intuitive grasp of a problem. By integrating LLMs into the stream- liner generation process, we can potentially uncover unique and subtle patterns in the problem formulation and utilize them to speed up the solving process. To some extent, StreamLLM is closer related to a hand-crafted streamliner design by human experts rather than an automated bottom-up streamliner generation, the dominant strategy of the previous research [Spracklen et al., 2023].

Our system StreamLLM for streamliner generation combines queries to LLMs with quick em- pirical validation on small test instances solvable within seconds. The system generates effective constraints within minutes through engineered prompts and adaptive feedback based on stream- liner performance. This realtime approach allows streamliner benefits to be realized even for hard problem instances that would typically require hours to solve—any significant speedup can outweigh the brief time spent on streamliner generation.

We evaluate StreamLLM extensively across seven diverse constraint problems, including stan- dard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, using hundreds of instances spec- ified in MiniZinc. The problems are formulated in the MiniZinc constraint programming lan- guage [Nethercote et al., 2007]. We test StreamLLM with two state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet), different prompting variants, and different strategies for combining adaptive prompting with test runs on easy instances. Our results demonstrate remarkable effectiveness, with some streamliners reducing solving time by up to 99%.

To investigate whether these improvements stem from memorization or genuine problem un- derstanding, we test StreamLLM on disguised and obfuscated variants of the problems. The system’s continued strong performance, particularly on problems with no known streamliners in the literature, suggests real analytical capabilities rather than mere pattern matching.

Beyond the realtime setting, we explore offline approaches with extended training periods and larger instance sets, achieving even better results in some cases. We also investigate StreamLLM’s ability to generate effective combinations of streamliners, finding that this often outperforms single constraints. Analysis of the generated streamliners reveals an interesting mix—some mirror expert-designed constraints while others take novel yet highly effective approaches.

Our experimental results suggest that LLM-generated streamliners can meaningfully reduce runtime across diverse constraint satisfaction problems with minimal problem-specific tuning, pointing to promising applications of AI assistance in constraint programming for computationally challenging problems.

Part of this work appeared in preliminary and shortened form at the 1st International Workshop on Neuro-Symbolic Software Engineering (NSE) [Voboril et al., 2025c].

The source code and benchmark instances can be found on Zenodo [Voboril et al., 2025b].

Headline:  
Large Language Models Redefine Constraint Programming via Autonomous Streamliner Generation

News Article:  
Recent research in combinatorial optimization has demonstrated the significant potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) to transcend the boundaries imposed by traditional algorithms based solely on mathematical reasoning. In engineering domains, classical combinatorial optimization (CO) approaches are often limited in their capacity to capture contextual nuances, particularly when domain-specific information or structural knowledge is indispensable for achieving high-quality solutions. The emergence of LLMs, capable of synthesizing both mathematical and contextual domain knowledge, has catalyzed tangible advances in key applications. This paradigm is exemplified by recent studies on Design Structure Matrix (DSM) sequencing, where LLM-powered frameworks that integrate network topology and semantic insights have consistently yielded faster convergence rates and superior solution quality compared to benchmark methods ([Anchor Event]; Proceedings of the Design Society).

Built on this evolving landscape, the latest work by Voboril et al. [1] represents a technical breakthrough in the automated synthesis of streamliners for constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs) formalized in the MiniZinc modeling language. Traditionally, streamliners—specialized constraints that strategically reduce the solution space—have required either substantial expert intervention or the systematic, brute-force composition and empirical testing of atomic constraints. Manual streamliner design leverages the researcher’s theoretical understanding of the domain, often resulting in constraints that precisely guide the solver to bypass inferior regions of the search space. Automated methods, while scalable in certain respects, typically operate in a bottom-up fashion, combining atomic predicates and validating them over large instance sets through intensive offline analysis.

Voboril et al. present a neuro-symbolic methodology, StreamLLM, that engages LLMs—specifically GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet—to autonomously generate candidate streamliners from MiniZinc formulations, followed by rapid empirical validation on tractable problem instances. This mechanism explicitly mirrors the hybrid reasoning strategy outlined in the DSM optimization literature—combining domain-centric semantic cues with mathematical constraints to optimize both search efficiency and solution quality. The analog to DSM research is direct and technically grounded: just as LLMs in those contexts leverage integrated knowledge to accelerate convergence and enhance outcomes, StreamLLM demonstrates that LLMs can propose highly effective streamliners that deliver substantial runtime reductions, often eclipsing both manually-crafted and conventionally automated strategies.

Crucially, the system’s workflow is designed to elicit streamliners through iterative LLM prompting, where feedback from initial test runs is incorporated into new constraint proposals. This tight feedback loop enables the discovery and empirical vetting of streamliners within minutes, a process that would otherwise necessitate extensive manual tuning or exorbitant offline computation. Empirical validation was rigorously executed across seven diverse benchmark CSPs—including problems with no prior art on effective streamliners—using hundreds of instance variations encoded in MiniZinc. The resulting runtime reductions, in some cases approaching 99%, attest to the LLM’s capability to not only emulate expert reasoning but also to synthesize nontrivial constraint combinations.

To probe the origins of these improvements, the research subjects StreamLLM to obfuscated and disguised variants of benchmark problems, mitigating the possibility of superficial pattern memorization. The persistence of significant performance gains, even under these adversarial conditions, substantiates the assertion that LLMs engage in genuine symbolic and semantic reasoning rather than mere pattern matching. This echoes the DSM research finding that LLMs’ ability to combine domain context with formal structure is not dependent on memorization but rather on their neuro-symbolic integration pipeline.

Further analysis extends to the compositionality of streamliners. While traditional approaches often assess atomic or individual constraints, StreamLLM can generate and validate complex combinations of streamliners. This characteristic, observed to outperform single-constraint approaches in several scenarios, underscores the model’s capacity to uncover nuanced, context-aware constraint ensembles that classical methods are unlikely to identify efficiently. The resulting streamliners reflect a spectrum: some closely parallel expert-crafted constraints documented in the literature, while others exemplify innovative approaches previously unconsidered in human-guided or systematized automated work.

From a broader perspective, the technical implications are twofold. First, LLM-driven streamliner generation can meaningfully and autonomously reduce computational resource expenditure across a variety of CSPs, as evidenced by extensive empirical evaluation involving over 310 CPU days. Second, the integration of LLMs into established constraint programming pipelines, particularly through tight empirical feedback, paves a viable path for neuro-symbolic collaboration in the solution of computationally intensive problems. The DSM research context provides a robust real-world anchor: the hybridization of semantic and mathematical reasoning pathways, as operationalized by LLMs in engineering optimization, is validated here at the core of constraint satisfaction speedup and generalizability.

Researchers and practitioners targeting next-generation CSP solvers—and more broadly, AI-augmented computational design pipelines—are invited to access the full research paper ([Voboril et al., 2025](https://dx.doi.org/10.1613/jair.1.18965)) along with source code and benchmark instances available through Zenodo. The empirical and technical artifacts presented therein constitute an invaluable resource for advancing both the theory and practice of neuro-symbolic optimization.

anchor_event.full_content:
Combinatorial optimization (CO) is essential for improving efficiency and performance in engineering applications. Traditional algorithms based on pure mathematical reasoning are limited and incapable to capture the contextual nuances for optimization. This study explores the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) in solving engineering CO problems by leveraging their reasoning power and contextual knowledge. We propose a novel LLM-based framework that integrates network topology and contextual domain knowledge to optimize the sequencing of Design Structure Matrix (DSM) —a common CO problem. Our experiments on various DSM cases demonstrate that the proposed method achieves faster convergence and higher solution quality than benchmark methods. Moreover, results show that incorporating contextual domain knowledge significantly improves performance despite the choice of LLMs.

Title: Large language models for combinatorial optimization of design structure matrix | Proceedings of the Design Society | Cambridge Core

Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated their powerful capabilities in natural language generation, semantic understanding, instruction following, and complex reasoning ([Reference Wei, Yi, Rishi, Colin, Barret, Sebastian and Dani Wei, et al. 2022](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr31)) ([Reference Chang, Xu, Jindong, Yuan, Linyi, Kaijie and Hao Chang, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr7)). Furthermore, pioneering studies have shown that LLMs can be used for continuous and concrete optimization ([Reference Romera-Paredes, Mohammadamin, Alexander, Matej, Pawan, Emilien and Francisco Romera-Paredes, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr28)) ([Reference Yang, Xuezhi, Yifeng, Hanxiao, Quoc Le, Denny and Xinyun Yang, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr33)) ([Reference Liu, Caishun, Xinghua, Ke and Yew-Soon Liu, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr19)). For instance, researchers from DeepMind utilized LLMs as optimizers and evaluated their effectiveness on classic CO problems ([Reference Yang, Xuezhi, Yifeng, Hanxiao, Quoc Le, Denny and Xinyun Yang, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr33)), such as Traveling Salesman Problems (TSP). Additionally, prior studies also highlight that LLMs possess extensive domain knowledge pretrained across a wide range of engineering-related data, which enhances their applicability in engineering fields ([Reference Zhu and Jianxi Zhu and Luo 2023](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr35)) ([Reference Makatura, Michael, Bohan, Felix, Pingchuan, Bolei and Megan Makatura, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr21)) ([Reference Jiang and Jianxi Jiang and Luo 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr17)) ([Reference Mei, Guo-Niu, Huaxiang and Zhongxue.Mei, et al. 2024](https://resolve.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-design-society/article/large-language-models-for-combinatorial-optimization-of-design-structure-matrix/51E70ED88E0E883492C512330E12403C/core-reader#bibr22)). Therefore, the ability of LLMs to combine mathematical and semantic reasoning, along with their possession of extensive knowledge, motivated us to explore their potential for solving engineering CO problems while integrating contextual domain knowledge relevant to their network typology. Our hypotheses are: (1) LLMs can be effectively applied to solve CO problems in engineering, and (2) incorporating contextual domain knowledge can further enhance LLM performance by supporting mathematical reasoning with semantic insights. This paradigm, which leverages both semantic and mathematical reasoning, introduces a novel approach to combinatorial optimization that traditional pure mathematical methods cannot achieve for empirical problems. On this basis, we propose a novel LLM-based framework that integrates both network topology and domain context into the optimization process.

title: Enabling small language models to solve complex reasoning tasks
abstract: Abstract While test-time reasoning enables language models (LMs) to tackle com- plex tasks, searching or planning in natural language can be slow, costly, and error-prone. But even when LMs struggle to emulate the precise rea- soning steps needed to solve a problem, they often excel at describing its abstract structure—both how to verify solutions and how to search for them. This paper introduces DISCIPL, a method for “self-steering” LMs where a Planner model generates a task-specific inference program that is executed by a population of Follower models. Our approach equips LMs with the abil- ity to write recursive search procedures that guide LM inference, enabling new forms of verifiable and efficient reasoning. When instantiated with a small Follower (e.g., Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-1.7B), DISCIPL matches (and sometimes outperforms) much larger models, including GPT-4o and o1, on challenging constrained generation tasks. Our work opens up a design space of highly-parallelized Monte Carlo inference strategies that outperform standard best-of-N sampling, require no finetuning, and can be implemented automatically by existing LMs.
introduction:
Introduction

Even as language models (LMs) are becoming increasingly proficient at reasoning tasks, progress has been “jagged” (Karpathy, 2024; Roose, 2025): today’s frontier models surpass experts at science and math reasoning (e.g., Hendrycks et al., 2021; Rein et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b) yet still routinely struggle with counting, arithmetic, tic-tac-toe, metered po- etry, and other intuitively simple tasks (Ball et al., 2024; McCoy et al., 2023; Xu & Ma, 2024). For example, even very capable LMs have difficulty writing a coherent sentence under the constraints in Fig. 1, which are manageable for most proficient English speakers.

There is a growing consensus that many problems require a more deliberate, effortful style of thinking (Kahneman, 2011). However, an open question is how to structure inference so as to best leverage available computational resources. One popular approach performs in-context reasoning via serialized chain-of-thought (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2024b). While highly flexible, reasoning via autoregressive generation is costly, slow, and can still produce unreliable outputs. On the other hand, structured inference methods like tree search (Silver et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2023) and sequential Monte Carlo (Lew et al., 2023; Loula et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2024) attain better parallelism and efficiency by coordinating test-time computation via external algorithms. However, these methods require significant hand-engineering and rely on pre-defined scorers or verifiers, limiting their applicability.

In this work, we propose a new meta-reasoning framework called DISCIPL in which lan- guage models themselves drive the decisions for how to structure inference-time computa- tion. In our approach, a Planner LM generates an ad-hoc problem specification (encoding its understanding of the task requirements) and inference procedure (encoding its plan for how to solve the task). Importantly, the specification and plan are implemented as inference programs that invoke Follower LMs, either generatively or as likelihood evaluators. By decomposing reasoning into planning and execution, our architecture preserves flexibility while enabling orchestration of highly efficient, parallel search patterns.

Requires non-trivial search; standard LMs struggle to satisfy all constraints.

Figure 1: Self-steering language models with probabilistic programs. LMs struggle with problems that combine generation and reasoning under constraints (top left, task from Yao et al., 2024). Popular approaches (top right) scale inference by search or sampling; however, even costly reasoning models do not always yield correct or fluent results (CoT example from o1, single-shot, first attempt). In our method (DISCIPL, bottom), a Planner LM writes an inference program that defines step-by-step com- putations to steer a population of Follower LMs. Our approach combines the benefits of serial and parallel methods: the Planner ensures correctness by construction, while the Followers collectively search for sequences with high probability. (See Fig. 7 for a detailed visualization.)

To test this approach, we evaluate DISCIPL on two domains: (1) COLLIE (Yao et al., 2024), a challenging constrained generation benchmark on which even very large LMs perform unreliably; and (2) PUZZLES, a custom dataset of difficult tasks involving poetry compo- sition, grant-writing, budgeting, and itinerary planning. We instantiate DISCIPL using a capable Planner LM to generate code (GPT-4o prompted with few-shot examples), and a small Follower LM to execute it (lightweight versions of Llama-3 or Qwen3). On para- graph tasks, this approach boosts accuracy well beyond the original capabilities of the Follower. Meanwhile, on more tightly constrained sentence and puzzle tasks, DISCIPL enables the Follower to surpass the Planner and approach the performance of powerful reasoning models like o1. Finally, we study how DISCIPL scales with the size and capabil- ities of the underlying Follower model, allowing our approach to benefit organically from advances in LM architecture and training methods.

Amidst a growing body of task-specific search and sampling methods for LMs (§2), we introduce a generalized approach. By using LMs to implement their own inference pro- cedures on-the-fly, DISCIPL yields efficient solutions to problems that would otherwise require non-trivial human engineering. We believe that this new combination of code gen- eration and probabilistic inference represents an exciting step in test-time scaling.

Headline:  
Self-Steering Language Models Surpass Limiting Constraints: DISCIPL’s Programmatic Approach to Inference

News Article:  

Despite steady advances in language models’ performance, persistent deficiencies in complex, constrained reasoning tasks continue to expose fundamental limitations. Tasks like Sudoku remain prohibitive: language models (LMs) either fail to fill in grids consistent with strict rules or execute inefficient trial-and-error procedures. As highlighted recently, even large LMs exhibit difficulty when asked to generate full solutions for problems where every step must conform to prespecified constraints. Current LMs may reliably verify user-completed Sudoku boards, but cannot autonomously construct legal solutions—underscoring the enduring gap between high-level reasoning and robust, rule-following generation (see [Real-World Anchor Event]).

The DISCIPL (Distributional Constraints by Inference Programming with Language Models) framework directly addresses these deficits by providing a meta-reasoning architecture that enables LMs to proactively structure and steer their own inference-time computations. Rather than relying solely on serialized chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning—which, even in powerful models, either incurs high computational cost or yields unreliable results—DISCIPL delegates the inferential process itself to LMs, allowing them to generate bespoke probabilistic programs that coordinate a population of subordinate language models on-the-fly ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]).

This capability is crucial for disambiguating the two complementary roles an LM may play: it may be more adept at describing the strategy to solve a task—including how to verify solutions and enumerate them—than at actually performing the tightly-constrained generation step itself. DISCIPL exploits this representational advantage by bifurcating inference into planning and execution, instantiated respectively by “Planner” and “Follower” LMs. The Planner model formulates a task-specific program—encoding rules, admissible actions, and validation logic—which is then executed by one or several smaller Follower LMs, whose collective search is guided via recursive, dynamically-allocated calls ([Abstract], Figure 1).

Traditional approaches to inference under constraints—ranging from best-of-N sampling to explicit tree search and sequential Monte Carlo—either depend on substantial human engineering, pre-defined verifiers, or incur serial bottlenecks that impede scalable parallel search ([Introduction]). DISCIPL’s architecture avoids these limitations: the Planner LM is responsible for programmatically encoding both the problem specification and an executable, recursive inference plan. These programs can be written and orchestrated by LMs without external tuning or manual intervention, subsequently executed by Followers in parallel to achieve efficient coverage over candidate solutions.

In empirical evaluation, DISCIPL demonstrates a marked advance over established inference paradigms. On the COLLIE benchmark—designed to test constrained generation under strict rules—DISCIPL, when instantiated with a Planner such as GPT-4o and light Follower models (Llama-3.2-1B, Qwen3-1.7B), outperforms or matches the accuracy of much larger models, including state-of-the-art reasoning LMs like o1 ([Abstract]). The framework closes the gap between model scale and inference quality: by leveraging its decomposition, it lifts the effective reasoning capabilities of small LMs to parity with larger, expensive systems. This paradigm shift is further supported by evaluation on the PUZZLES dataset, which includes tasks on poetry composition, grant-writing, budgeting, and itinerary planning. DISCIPL permits tightly-constrained generation, enabling Followers to exceed even the Planner’s one-shot performance in highly rule-bound tasks ([Introduction]).

Technically, DISCIPL expands the design space available to LM-driven inference. Its meta-reasoning programs instantiate highly-parallelized, Monte Carlo-style search processes that are verifiable by construction and leverage existing LM APIs, without need for further finetuning. The separation of planning (program synthesis) from execution (multi-agent search) allows the system to dynamically scale with both task complexity and LM improvements, bridging observed deficiencies in both small and large models.

By empowering language models to autonomously write and execute their own search programs for constrained generation, DISCIPL directly counters the inefficiencies and error rates highlighted in real-world deployments. The paper’s synthesis of programmatic planning and scalable probabilistic inference represents an actionable advance in test-time scaling, offering a systematic alternative to both ad-hoc sampling and manual engineering.

For researchers and practitioners seeking a rigorous framework to close the gap between LM architectures and practical task fidelity, direct engagement with the full paper is recommended: https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.07081.

anchor_event.full_content:
Title: Enabling small language models to solve complex reasoning tasks

Markdown Content:
As language models (LMs) improve at tasks like image generation, trivia questions, and simple math, you might think that human-like reasoning is around the corner. In reality, they still trail us by a wide margin on complex tasks. Try playing Sudoku with one, for instance, where you fill in numbers one through nine in such a way that each appears only once across the columns, rows, and sections of a nine-by-nine grid. Your AI opponent will either fail to fill in boxes on its own or do so inefficiently, although it can verify if you’ve filled yours out correctly.

Whether an LM is trying to solve advanced puzzles, design molecules, or write math proofs, the system struggles to answer open-ended requests that have strict rules to follow. The model is better at telling users how to approach these challenges than attempting them itself. Moreover, hands-on problem-solving requires LMs to consider a wide range of options while following constraints. Small LMs can’t do this reliably on their own; large language models (LLMs) sometimes can, particularly if they’re optimized for reasoning tasks, but they take a while to respond, and they use a lot of computing power.

title: AI agents debate their way to improved mathematical reasoning
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved impressive results in complex reasoning and knowledge tasks, yet they often struggle with factual accuracy and logical consistency. Prior works have improved LLM performance using prompt-based techniques (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting and self-consistency) and post-hoc self-refinement, but these typically operate on a single model instance. Recently, multi-agent debate frameworks have emerged as a complementary approach, wherein multiple LLM agents propose answers and critique each other’s reasoning to reach consensus. Such a “society of minds” approach has been shown to significantly improve mathematical reasoning and reduce factual hallucinations. However, existing debate methods use homogeneous agents with simple majority voting, limiting their effectiveness. In this work, we propose Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD), a novel framework that extends multi-agent debate with (i) diverse specialized agents, (ii) dynamic debate routing, and (iii) a learned consensus mechanism. Each agent in A-HMAD is assigned a distinct role or expertise (e.g. logical reasoning, factual verification, strategic planning), enabling more comprehensive error-checking and perspective diversity than identical agents. A coordination policy dynamically selects which agents contribute at each round based on the question’s domain and the evolving debate state. To aggregate viewpoints, we introduce a consensus optimizer that learns to weight each agent’s vote according to its reliability and the confidence of its arguments. On six challenging benchmarks – including arithmetic QA, grade-school math (GSM8K), multifact question answering (MMLU), factual biography generation, and chess strategy – our A-HMAD consistently outperforms prior single-model methods and the original multi-agent debate baseline. Notably, A-HMAD achieves 4–6% absolute accuracy gains over standard debate on these tasks, and reduces factual errors by over 30% in biography facts. We provide extensive ablations demonstrating the benefits of agent heterogeneity, additional debate rounds, and the learned consensus module. Our findings suggest that an adaptive, role-diverse debating ensemble can drive significant advances in LLM-based educational reasoning, paving the way for safer, more interpretable, and pedagogically reliable AI systems.
introduction: Large-scale pre-trained language models have revolutionized NLP, demonstrating remarkable prowess in tasks requiring knowledge and reasoning (Brown et al. 2020; Srivastava et al. 2023). Models like GPT-3 and its successors exhibit strong few-shot learning abilities (Brown et al. 2020) and can generate coherent multi-step reasoning chains when prompted appropriately (Kojima et al. 2022). Despite these gains, even state-of-the-art LLMs often produce hallucinations – confidently stated false facts – and illogical reasoning leaps, undermining their reliability (Lin et al. 2021; Ji et al. 2023). This is due in part to the noisy, unverified nature of web-scale training data and the lack of explicit reasoning verification in standard next-token prediction training (Ouyang et al. 2022; Ziegler et al. 2019). Improving the factual accuracy and reasoning soundness of LLM outputs has therefore become a pressing research goal (Lee et al. 2022; Kadavath et al. 2022). A rich body of recent work has explored techniques to mitigate LLM reasoning and factual errors. Prompting strategies such as providing step-by-step exemplars or eliciting a chain-of-thought (CoT) have led to notable improvements in arithmetic and commonsense reasoning (Kojima et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). For example, Wei et al. (2022) showed that CoT prompting enables models to break down problems into logical steps, and Kojima et al. (2022) found that even zero-shot prompts (e.g. “Let’s think step by step”) can induce multi-step reasoning in LLMs. Building on CoT, self-consistency decoding samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the most common answer, which boosts correctness by averaging out reasoning variance (Wang et al. 2022). Other works equip LLMs with scratchpads or external tools: Nye et al. (2021) introduced “scratchpad” memory for intermediate computations, and tool-use approaches allow models to consult calculators or knowledge bases to check facts (Yao et al. 2023; Schick et al. 2023). Orthogonal to prompting, learning-from-feedback methods like reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) fine-tune models to favor truthful, helpful outputs (Christiano et al. 2017; Stiennon et al. 2020), significantly reducing toxic or untrue generations (Siddiky et al. 2025). Likewise, post-hoc verifier models have been trained to check and correct a model’s answers (e.g. for math word problems Cobbe et al., 2021). Another promising direction is self-reflection and refinement, where the model iteratively critiques and improves its own output (Kawamura et al. 2003; Madaan et al. 2023). Madaan et al. (2023) showed that prompting an LLM to reflect on potential errors and then attempt a revised solution can yield more accurate results, echoing human problem-solving strategies (Renze and Guven 2024). Despite these advances, most techniques above involve a single model reasoning in isolation; the model may still overlook certain errors or fail to verify all facts due to its inherent biases or blind spots (Kadavath et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). In contrast to single-model approaches, multi-agent frameworks draw inspiration from social or argumentative processes in which multiple experts collaborate or debate to reach a better answer (Michael et al. 2023). Early conceptual work by Minsky posited that intelligent behavior could emerge from a “Society of Mind” – a diverse collection of simpler agents. In the context of AI alignment and truthfulness, Khan et al. (2024) proposed AI debate, where two agents argue for opposing answers and a judge (human or AI) decides which argument is more convincing. The hope is that through adversarial debate, the truth would prevail because any false argument can be revealed by the opponent (Khan et al. 2024). While this work was theoretical, recent empirical studies have started to realize the benefits of multi-agent interactions. For example, Li et al. (2023) introduced a communicative multi-agent system (Camel) where agents with different roles (user, assistant, critic) collaborate to solve tasks. Similarly, MetaGPT by Hong et al. (2023) uses multiple specialized agent personas (e.g. PM, engineer, tester) to decompose and solve complex programming tasks, showing the power of role specialization. Focusing on language tasks, Kadavath et al. (2022) demonstrated that allowing two LLMs to debate from different viewpoints encourages divergent thinking and can improve answer quality. Parrish et al. (2022) found that when a less truthful LLM “debates” a more truthful LLM, the resulting answers tend to be more factual, suggesting that persuasion by a knowledgeable agent can correct the other’s misconceptions. These works hint that multi-agent interaction might overcome individual model limitations. However, some studies report mixed results; Chan et al. (2023) showed that a simple two-turn debate did not significantly help humans answer reading comprehension questions, implying that how debate is structured is critical. Most relevant to our work, Du et al. (2023) recently proposed a multi-agent debate framework for LLMs to improve both reasoning and factuality. In their approach, multiple instances of the same LLM are each prompted to generate an answer with reasoning. The agents then exchange their answers and critique each other’s reasoning over several rounds, before outputting a final consensus answer. This method, requiring only black-box access to the models’ outputs, was shown to significantly enhance performance on math word problems, strategic games, and factual QA, compared to single-agent baselines. Notably, Du et al. reported that debate reduces hallucinations: agents often identify and remove one another’s uncertain or inconsistent facts, leading to a final answer with more reliable information. The multi-agent debate can be seen as inducing an ensemble-like effect where multiple reasoning paths are explored in parallel and vetted against each other, yielding higher confidence when they converge. Zhang et al. (2023) further applied a debating agents idea for LLM-based evaluation, showing that a multi-agent debate can refine judgment of answers. Wu et al. (2024) used debates between a “student” and “professor” model to supervise and improve unreliable reasoning, while Zelikman et al. (2022) leveraged cooperative agents for embodied decision-making. These underline a growing consensus that multi-agent interactions can enhance an LLM’s performance and reliability. Despite the promise of multi-agent debate, there remain clear opportunities for improvement. First, prior debate frameworks largely use homogeneous agents – e.g. identical GPT-3.5 models with the same prompting style. This limits the diversity of perspectives; if all agents share similar strengths and weaknesses, certain errors may go unchallenged. Recent works in multi-agent collaboration emphasize heterogeneity as a key factor for success (Li et al. 2023; Hong et al. 2023), which suggests that introducing specialized agents could further improve the debate outcome. Second, existing methods rely on a simple majority vote or unanimous agreement for the final answer. This treats all agents as equally reliable, ignoring the possibility that some agents (or certain argument lines) might be more likely correct than others. A more nuanced consensus mechanism that weights agents by their past accuracy or the confidence of their justifications could lead to more robust final answers. Third, the debate protocol in prior work is static – agents alternate in a fixed order and number of rounds. In complex scenarios, a rigid protocol might be suboptimal: for example, if a particular agent has expertise most relevant to the query, the framework should allocate more opportunity for that agent to contribute, while bypassing irrelevant agents to save computation. Such dynamic agent routing or adaptive scheduling of debate rounds has not been explored in existing work. Finally, no prior study (to our knowledge) has integrated an explicit learning component into the debate loop – the interplay is handled by prompting alone. This raises the question of whether parts of the multi-agent debate process (e.g. how to aggregate answers) can be learned from data to further boost performance. In this paper, we propose a new framework called Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) that addresses the above limitations and substantially improves LLM factuality and reasoning via multi-agent debate. Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We design a heterogeneous agent ensemble where each agent has a specialized role or distinct model type, along with a dynamic routing strategy that activates different subsets of agents depending on the query type and intermediate debate outcomes. (2) We propose a learned consensus module that replaces simple majority voting for final answer selection, weighting agents based on features of their responses and confidence. (3) We thoroughly evaluate A-HMAD on six challenging benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results for our base model, and significantly reducing hallucinations and incorrect facts compared to single-agent and prior debate baselines. (4) Through controlled ablations and qualitative case studies, we show why our components matter and how expert agents can correct others’ errors. Together, these advances move us closer to robust, truth-seeking AI systems that can reliably solve complex problems and self-correct errors.

The rapidly evolving domain of large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems (MAS) is redefining how artificial reasoning, factual verification, and consensus emerge from collections of autonomous LLM agents. Recent studies, such as those detailed by Guo et al. (2024), have highlighted the interplay between agent diversity, communication, and conflict resolution as pivotal factors driving the next generation of collective intelligence in LLM-based MAS. A particularly salient research trajectory focuses on how these systems orchestrate role-specialized agents and consensus mechanisms for collaborative problem-solving—including debate protocols, dynamic voting, and memory-enabled coordination (as synthesized in survey and technical anchoring documents [PDF] Unraveling the Consensus-Diversity Tradeoff in Adaptive Multi ...). Yet, as these techniques move beyond static, homogeneous ensembles or fixed interaction patterns, new frameworks that operationalize agent specialization, adaptive routing, and data-driven consensus optimization are emerging as critical advances in pursuit of interpretability, robustness, and truthfulness in LLMs.

Positioning itself directly within this evolving landscape, the newly proposed Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) framework represents a technical inflection point in LLM-based MAS, explicitly addressing several bottlenecks and open challenges identified in foundational work. Specifically, A-HMAD operationalizes three research imperatives discussed across both foundational and recent literature: (i) the integration of heterogeneous, role-specialized LLM agents; (ii) the implementation of dynamic debate routing based on query domain and evolving debate trajectories; and (iii) a learned consensus optimizer to appropriately weight agent contributions, rather than relying on simple majority voting. By aligning its architecture with ongoing scholarly discourse around consensus-diversity tradeoffs and autonomy-preserving agent interaction design (cf. Liu et al., 2024; Cho et al., 2024), A-HMAD crystallizes key methodological recommendations into a unified, empirically validated system.

A-HMAD’s design directly addresses limitations highlighted by prior multi-agent debate studies, such as those by Du et al. (2023), in which multiple instances of a homogeneous LLM were pitted against each other with fixed debate protocols and uniform voting. While these frameworks achieved measurable gains in reasoning accuracy and reduced factual hallucination compared to single-agent systems—by allowing adversarial critique and parallel exploration of reasoning paths—they were constrained by uniformity of agent expertise and static aggregation of opinions. The consensus-diversity tradeoff observed in [PDF] Unraveling the Consensus-Diversity Tradeoff in Adaptive Multi ... points to the importance of balancing viewpoint variety with effective convergence on reliable answers; total agent similarity curtails error correction, while unchecked autonomy may impede rapid consensus.

Within A-HMAD, each agent is instantiated with a distinct specialization—such as logical reasoning, factual verification, or strategic planning—mirroring “hierarchical roles” and ensemble perspectives documented as beneficial in systems like CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) and MAgICoRe (Chen et al., 2024c). This role heterogeneity enables comprehensive error-checking and supports the autonomy-preserving disagreement championed in recent MAS literature as vital for adaptability and knowledge consistency (Guo et al., 2024). The dynamic debate routing policy in A-HMAD further optimizes agent participation in real time, selectively activating agents based on the nature of the query and intermediate debates, rather than predefining interaction order or agent inclusion. This adaptivity is in direct conversation with findings by Wang et al. (2024) and similar studies, which report that the benefits of multi-agent interaction are highly contingent on both task complexity and adaptive protocol structure.

Central to A-HMAD’s advance over majority-voting paradigms is the introduction of a learned consensus optimizer. Departing from methods that treat all agents as equally reliable, this module weights agent contributions according to both their demonstrated reliability and the context-driven confidence of their arguments. Such mechanisms directly address the “knowledge consistency” challenge and majority illusion cautioned in recent surveys (Guo et al., 2024; Dippel et al., 2024), and extend beyond ensemble averaging or self-consistency protocols seen in prior LLM-based MAS research.

Empirical evaluation on six challenging benchmarks—spanning arithmetic QA, GSM8K, multifact QA (MMLU), factual biography generation, and chess strategy—demonstrates that A-HMAD consistently outperforms both single-model approaches and homogeneous multi-agent debate baselines. The framework delivers 4–6% absolute accuracy gains over conventional debate protocols and reduces factual errors in biography generation by more than 30%. Extensive ablation studies further confirm that each architectural innovation—agent heterogeneity, dynamic coordination, and the learned consensus module—contributes to measurable improvements in factuality, reasoning soundness, and task robustness.

In summary, A-HMAD operationalizes emerging design principles in LLM-based MAS by dynamically leveraging agent diversity, adaptive routing, and learning-based consensus mechanisms. It provides a robust, empirically validated blueprint for advancing educational reasoning, reliability, and interpretability in LLM systems. For researchers aiming to address the open question of scalable, autonomous truth-seeking in AI, the detailed methodology and findings in the A-HMAD research paper present an essential resource.  

To engage with the technical specifics, experimental results, and broader implications of this work, readers are urged to access the full paper at https://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00353-3.
